authors: Carbonaro, A., Giorgetti, L., Ridolfi, L., Pasolini, R., Pagliarani, A., Cavallucci, M., Andalò, A., Del Gaudio, L., De Angelis, P., Vespignani, R., Gentili, N.
title: From raw data to research-ready: A FHIR-based transformation pipeline in a real-world oncology setting
neural_component: No neural/deep learning component is described. The pipeline uses rule-based SPARQL transformations and ontology-driven reasoning without any neural model, embeddings, or pre-training.
symbolic_component: OWL-based FHIR ontology, RDF/RDFS constraints, domain ontology (CVL ontology), SPARQL INSERT queries for semantic alignment, GraphDB built-in reasoner with custom rulesets for OWL entailments (owl:equivalentClass, owl:subClassOf, rdfs:domain, rdfs:range). External biomedical terminologies: SNOMED CT, LOINC, ICD-10, ATC, NCIT, MedDRA, HUGO.
symbolic_inference_evidence: The symbolic component operates at multiple inference-time phases: (1) during the Validation phase, RDFS.domain and RDFS.range constraints from the OWL-based FHIR ontology are applied to validate property usage and data types; (2) during Graph Deployment, automated SPARQL INSERT queries assert rdf:type declarations and property alignments linking FHIR resources to CVL ontology concepts; (3) during Reasoning, GraphDB's built-in reasoner applies OWL entailments (owl:equivalentClass, owl:subClassOf, owl:inverseOf, rdfs:domain, rdfs:range) and custom rulesets to infer new classes (e.g., DrinkerPatient, SmokerPatient) from physiological anamnesis data.
component_interaction: A modular ETL pipeline transforms CSV data into FHIR JSON, then into FHIR RDF (Turtle). The symbolic ontology (OWL-based FHIR vocabulary and CVL domain ontology) is used for validation during conversion, and then SPARQL INSERT queries and OWL reasoning enrich the RDF graph post-loading into GraphDB. There is no neural component; the entire flow is symbolic: input → refinement → mapping (with terminology binding) → validation (ontology-based) → graph deployment → reasoning.
datasets: [{"name": "IRST IRCCS real-world oncology dataset", "size": "36,335 patients, 1,093,705 clinical records converted to 1,151,559 FHIR RDF resources", "domain": "Oncology"}]
metrics_best_result: 100% entity-level mapping correspondence across all 16 clinical entity types; ontology quality metrics: semantic accuracy 1.0 on all sub-metrics, consistency 1.0, intensional conciseness 1.0, extensional conciseness 0.97, defined classes compliance 0.96, defined properties compliance 0.62
baseline_gap: Not specified
explainability_mechanism: The pipeline provides traceability through the GraphDB Provenance plugin that facilitates tracing and verification of reasoning steps. SPARQL queries enable transparent inspection of ontology alignment and inferred triples. Human-readable labels (rdfs:label) are added to each FHIR resource for interpretability. Domain experts participated in validating semantic correctness and clinical relevance.
explainability_evaluation: Expert review was used to validate clinical relevance and technical correctness. Ontology quality metrics (KGHeartBeat-based) were computed via SPARQL queries. No formal user study on explainability.
hitl_details: Domain experts (clinical experts, data engineers, ontologists) collaborated to define mappings, validate semantic correctness, and ensure clinical relevance. This was part of the development/validation process rather than an algorithmic feedback loop. No details on iteration counts or time cost.
specific_sources: SNOMED CT, LOINC, ICD-10, ATC (Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical Classification), NCIT (National Cancer Institute Thesaurus), MedDRA, HUGO Gene Nomenclature, FHIR R4 OWL ontology (fhir.ttl), CVL domain ontology, KGHeartBeat
frameworks_libraries: Python, fhir.resources, fhirtordf, rdflib, Ontotext GraphDB, Docker, Qlik Sense
main_contribution: A modular FHIR-based transformation pipeline that converts large-scale, heterogeneous real-world oncology data (36,335 patients, >1M records) into semantically enriched RDF knowledge graphs with ontology-driven validation and reasoning capabilities.
limitations_reported: Tested and validated on data from a single institution only; dataset limited to deceased patients due to privacy constraints; ETL layer tightly coupled to local data environment; broader adoption requires regulatory adjustments, patient consent mechanisms, and multi-institutional validation; standard entailment rules incompatible with nested FHIR RDF structures requiring SPARQL INSERT workarounds; defined properties compliance metric of 0.62 reflects unused properties for not-yet-populated future classes.
relevance_to_thesis: Reusable pipeline architecture; KG/ontology component
free_notes: This article is primarily a data engineering/health informatics contribution focused on FHIR-to-RDF transformation with ontology-based validation and reasoning. It does not involve any neural component or machine learning model. The semantic matching is achieved through declarative mappings to standard biomedical terminologies and ontology-driven SPARQL queries, without neuro-symbolic integration. The 'reasoning' component uses OWL entailment and SPARQL INSERT rather than any learned representations.

authors: Xingsi Xue, Jerry Chun-Wei Lin, Tong Su
title: Compact Multitasking Multichromosome Genetic Algorithm for Heuristic Selection in Ontology Matching
source: IEEE Transactions on Artificial Intelligence, Vol. 5, No. 12
neural_component: Not applicable. The system uses a compact genetic algorithm (CGA) as the evolutionary optimization component, not a neural network. The evolutionary multitask optimization (EMO) framework with multichromosome encoding and adaptive knowledge transfer serves as the computational search mechanism.
symbolic_component: Ontology structures (hierarchical concept graphs with concepts, properties, and relationships); local consistency principle for entity assignment pruning based on hierarchical ontology constraints (superconcept/subconcept relationships); importance measures derived from social network analysis (degree, closeness, betweenness, eccentricity, stress centrality) applied to the ontology graph; symbolic similarity measures (SMOA string distance, Wu-Palmer distance via WordNet synsets, context distance, Jaccard coefficient).
symbolic_inference_evidence: The symbolic component operates at multiple inference phases: (1) Entity sequencing heuristics use graph-based importance measures (degree, closeness, betweenness, eccentricity, stress centrality) computed over the ontology's hierarchical graph to prioritize entities for matching. (2) Entity assignment pruning (Algorithm 3) enforces the local consistency principle at inference time, constraining candidate target entities based on superconcept/subconcept relationships from the ontology hierarchy. (3) Assigning heuristics use symbolic similarity measures (SMOA, Wu-Palmer via WordNet synsets, context distance, Jaccard coefficient) to compute entity similarity scores for mapping decisions. The genetic algorithm selects and combines these symbolic heuristics.
integration_mode: Other: Evolutionary metaheuristic selects/combines symbolic heuristics
component_interaction: The compact multitasking multichromosome genetic algorithm (CM2GA) acts as a hyperheuristic framework that selects and combines symbolic heuristics for two sub-tasks: entity sequencing (which entity to match next, using graph-based importance measures) and entity assignment (which target entity to pair with, using symbolic similarity measures). Multichromosome encoding represents the selection of sequencing and assigning heuristics separately. The evolutionary search optimizes heuristic combinations using a compact probability vector representation, while adaptive knowledge transfer across multiple constructed tasks (varying precision-recall trade-off weights) helps escape local optima. The ontology's hierarchical structure constrains candidate assignments via a local consistency pruning step.
datasets: [{"name": "OAEI Benchmark 2016", "size": "32 test cases (ontology pairs with reference alignments)", "domain": "general"}]
metrics_best_result: F-measure = 1.00 on multiple test cases (101, 221-247), F-measure = 0.99 on test case 249; mean F-measure across all 32 test cases consistently surpasses all compared methods
baseline_score: Perfect on many easy cases but drops significantly on hard cases (e.g., 0.16-0.89 on cases 248-262)
baseline_gap: CM2GA achieves +0.72 pp on test case 262 vs Lily (0.88 vs 0.16), substantial improvements on challenging cases
explainability_mechanism: N/A
explainability_evaluation: N/A
specific_sources: WordNet (used via Wu-Palmer distance-based similarity measure for linguistic similarity); SMOA string metric; Jaccard coefficient for instance-based similarity; graph centrality measures (degree, closeness, betweenness, eccentricity, stress) for entity importance
frameworks_libraries: Not specified
main_contribution: A compact multitasking multichromosome genetic algorithm (CM2GA) that uses evolutionary optimization to select and combine entity sequencing and assignment heuristics for ontology matching, with adaptive knowledge transfer across multiple constructed tasks to improve alignment quality and efficiency.
limitations_reported: The authors note the need to explore scalability to larger and more complex ontologies, validate performance in real-world OM scenarios across diverse domains, and incorporate domain-specific knowledge, semantic constraints, and external resources (semantic repositories, domain-specific ontologies) to enhance matching capabilities.
relevance_to_thesis: Reusable pipeline architecture; KG/ontology component; Evaluation metric/protocol
free_notes: This article is purely symbolic/evolutionary in nature with no neural component. The approach uses a compact genetic algorithm as a hyperheuristic to select among predefined symbolic similarity measures and graph-based importance measures for ontology matching. The multitasking framework constructs multiple related tasks by varying the precision-recall trade-off weight alpha. Extensive ablation studies compare variants removing sequencing heuristics, assigning heuristics, compact encoding, and different knowledge transfer strategies. The OAEI Benchmark test cases involve relatively small ontologies.

authors: Daniel Obraczka, Erhard Rahm
title: Comparing Symbolic and Embedding-Based Approaches for Relational Blocking
neural_component: Pre-trained word embeddings (fastText with SIF aggregation), Sentence-BERT (GTR-T5 for monolingual, LaBSE for multilingual), DeepBlocker variants (Autoencoder, Cross-Tuple Training, Hybrid), LightEA (KGE baseline), HDBSCAN clustering on embeddings, UMAP for dimensionality reduction
symbolic_component: Token Blocking based on symbolic token overlap, importance-based attribute/relation selection using support and discriminability metrics (from MinoanER), composite blocking scheme combining attribute blocking and relational blocking functions
symbolic_inference_evidence: At inference time, the symbolic component operates in two phases: (1) the importance scoring of attributes and relations uses symbolic support/discriminability metrics to select the most informative attributes and neighbor relations for each entity; (2) Token Blocking uses symbolic token overlap to assign entities sharing common tokens into the same blocks. In hybrid variants (RelTB_AC, RelTB_TC), entities assigned to the noise cluster by HDBSCAN fall back to symbolic Token Blocking. The composite blocking scheme (Equation 1) combines attribute blocking and relational blocking via disjunction.
integration_mode: Other: Composite blocking framework spanning neuro-symbolic spectrum with hybrid variants
component_interaction: The framework uses a composite blocking scheme Θ = Θ_A ∨ Θ_R, where Θ_A (attribute blocking) and Θ_R (relational blocking) can each be instantiated with symbolic (Token Blocking), neural (DeepBlocker, embeddings + kNN), or hybrid methods. In hybrid variants (RelTB_AC, RelTB_TC), token/attribute embeddings are clustered via HDBSCAN; entities in the noise cluster fall back to symbolic Token Blocking. RelTB_DeepBlocker uses Token Blocking for Θ_A and DeepBlocker embeddings with kNN for Θ_R. Importance scoring (symbolic) selects top attributes and relations before any blocking function is applied.
datasets: [{"name": "S-DW1 (DBpedia-Wikidata)", "size": "15k entities per KG", "domain": "general (Wikipedia-derived KGs)"}, {"name": "S-DW2 (DBpedia-Wikidata)", "size": "15k entities per KG", "domain": "general"}, {"name": "S-DY1 (DBpedia-YAGO)", "size": "15k entities per KG", "domain": "general"}, {"name": "S-DY2 (DBpedia-YAGO)", "size": "15k entities per KG", "domain": "general"}, {"name": "L-DW1 (DBpedia-Wikidata)", "size": "100k entities per KG", "domain": "general"}, {"name": "L-DW2 (DBpedia-Wikidata)", "size": "100k entities per KG", "domain": "general"}, {"name": "L-DY1 (DBpedia-YAGO)", "size": "100k entities per KG", "domain": "general"}, {"name": "L-DY2 (DBpedia-YAGO)", "size": "100k entities per KG", "domain": "general"}, {"name": "S-ED1 (DBpedia EN-DE)", "size": "15k entities per KG", "domain": "general, multilingual"}, {"name": "S-ED2 (DBpedia EN-DE)", "size": "15k entities per KG", "domain": "general, multilingual"}, {"name": "S-EF1 (DBpedia EN-FR)", "size": "15k entities per KG", "domain": "general, multilingual"}, {"name": "S-EF2 (DBpedia EN-FR)", "size": "15k entities per KG", "domain": "general, multilingual"}, {"name": "L-ED1 (DBpedia EN-DE)", "size": "100k entities per KG", "domain": "general, multilingual"}, {"name": "L-ED2 (DBpedia EN-DE)", "size": "100k entities per KG", "domain": "general, multilingual"}, {"name": "L-EF1 (DBpedia EN-FR)", "size": "100k entities per KG", "domain": "general, multilingual"}, {"name": "L-EF2 (DBpedia EN-FR)", "size": "100k entities per KG", "domain": "general, multilingual"}]
metrics_best_result: Recall = 100.0% on S-DW2 and S-DY2 (RelTB_TC with SIF); best h3r values achieved by RelTB_AC or RelTB_TC on all 16 tasks; RelTB_AC with SentenceTransformer best balance of Recall and Reduction Ratio
baseline_score: Outperformed by all other approaches on h3r
baseline_gap: Significant (Bayesian signed rank test, ≥95% posterior probability)
explainability_mechanism: N/A
explainability_evaluation: N/A
specific_sources: DBpedia, Wikidata, YAGO (as input KGs); fastText pre-trained word embeddings (SIF aggregation); Sentence-BERT (GTR-T5 for monolingual, LaBSE for multilingual); LightEA (KGE method)
frameworks_libraries: klinker (custom Python library), Faiss, RAPIDS (HDBSCAN, UMAP), NLTK, fastText, Sentence-BERT, Autorank, PyTorch (implied)
main_contribution: A generalized framework for relational blocking across the neuro-symbolic spectrum that enhances state-of-the-art blocking approaches with neighborhood information from KGs, showing that symbolic and hybrid methods significantly outperform deep learning-based blockers.
limitations_reported: Benchmark datasets have limited heterogeneity (all derived from Wikipedia); unrealistic 1-to-1 matching scenario; on some datasets no approach achieved acceptable blocking quality (e.g., at best 81% Recall on L-EF1); more realistic benchmark datasets are needed.
relevance_to_thesis: Reusable pipeline architecture; Transferable embedding component; KG/ontology component; Multilingual case; Reusable benchmark; Evaluation metric/protocol; NeSy taxonomy/classification
free_notes: The study explicitly positions its contributions along the neuro-symbolic spectrum. The composite blocking scheme generalizes MinoanER. Bayesian signed rank tests ensure statistical rigor. Three languages involved: English, German, French. The hybrid RelTB_AC/RelTB_TC variants combining symbolic token blocking with embedding clustering are identified as promising underexplored directions.

authors: Jifang Wu, Jianghua Lv, Haoming Guo, Shilong Ma
title: DAEOM: A Deep Attentional Embedding Approach for Biomedical Ontology Matching
neural_component: Siamese graph attention-based autoencoder (SGAT) combined with a BERT-based terminological description encoder (TBERT). TBERT uses a modified BERT with siamese networks and mean pooling to produce fixed-size terminological description embeddings. SGAT is a variant of Graph Attention Networks (GAT) that encodes both network structure and terminological description. Word vectors initialized from pre-trained PubMed+PMC+Wikipedia embeddings (dim=200).
symbolic_component: Ontology network structure (adjacency matrices encoding subClassOf relationships), rdfs:label terminological descriptions, inter-intra negative sampling constrained by ontological structural relations (subClassOf transitivity, parent/child/sibling neighborhoods). The proximity matrix P incorporates 1-order (parents/children) and 2-order (siblings) neighborhood structure from the ontology.
symbolic_inference_evidence: The ontology structure (subClassOf hierarchy encoded as adjacency matrices) is used at inference time in two places: (1) the SGAT encoder uses the proximity matrix P derived from ontology structural relations to weight attention coefficients during graph attention computation, directly shaping the learned embeddings; (2) the mapping selection module uses an iterative greedy + Stable Marriage algorithm with automatic threshold adjustment to select 1:1 correspondences from the similarity matrix computed over jointly-encoded embeddings.
component_interaction: TBERT first produces terminological description embeddings from rdfs:labels. These embeddings serve as input node features to the SGAT, which jointly encodes them with the ontology adjacency structure through graph attention layers. The total loss function jointly optimizes the reconstruction loss (preserving ontology topology) and the matching loss (cross-entropy over positive/negative inter-ontology mapping pairs). Negative sampling is constrained by ontological structural relations (excluding subClassOf-related concepts). The resulting latent representations are used by the mapping selection module to compute pairwise euclidean distances and select final 1:1 alignments via an improved Stable Marriage algorithm with automatic threshold.
datasets: [{"name": "OAEI Anatomy Track (MA-NCI)", "size": "2737 + 3298 concepts", "domain": "biomedical (anatomy)"}, {"name": "OAEI Large BioMed Track FMA-NCI (Small)", "size": "3696 + 6488 concepts", "domain": "biomedical"}, {"name": "OAEI Large BioMed Track FMA-SNOMED (Small)", "size": "10157 + 13412 concepts", "domain": "biomedical"}]
metrics_best_result: F1 = 0.936 on FMA-NCI (Small); F1 = 0.879 on FMA-SNOMED (Small); F1 = 0.849 on MA-NCI
baseline_score: F1 = 0.943 on MA-NCI, F1 = 0.933 on FMA-NCI, F1 = 0.835 on FMA-SNOMED
baseline_gap: +0.3 pp on FMA-NCI, +4.4 pp on FMA-SNOMED vs AML; -9.4 pp on MA-NCI vs AML
explainability_mechanism: N/A
explainability_evaluation: N/A
specific_sources: BERT (pre-trained), word vectors pre-trained on PubMed+PMC+English Wikipedia, OAEI biomedical ontologies (MA, NCI, FMA, SNOMED CT), UMLS Metathesaurus (reference alignments), rdfs:label and subClassOf ontology structure
frameworks_libraries: Python, TensorFlow
main_contribution: Proposes DAEOM, a siamese graph attention-based autoencoder framework that jointly encodes ontology terminological descriptions (via BERT) and network structure (via GAT) for biomedical ontology matching, with a novel inter-intra negative sampling strategy and automatic threshold adjustment for final alignment.
limitations_reported: Only considers subClassOf relationships, ignoring other relation types (e.g., property relationships); only tested on small ontology matching scenarios without graph segmentation or distributed training; lower recall than top OAEI systems due to not using domain-specific biomedical lexicons for synonym extraction.
relevance_to_thesis: Reusable pipeline architecture; Transferable embedding component; KG/ontology component; Evaluation metric/protocol
free_notes: DAEOM outperforms OAEI 2019 top-ranked systems on FMA-NCI and FMA-SNOMED in F-measure but underperforms AML on the MA-NCI Anatomy track. The automatic threshold method replaces manual tuning. The ablation study systematically compares TBERT, SGAT, and negative sampling components, as well as alternative graph encoders (GraphSAGE, TransE).

authors: Centelles Velilla, M., & Ferran-Ferrer, N.
title: Using Wikidata's Ontology in Practice: A Neuro-Symbolic, Community-Centred Workflow for Integrating and Reusing Humanities Datasets
source: Journal of Open Humanities Data, 11: 84, pp. 1–14
neural_component: LLM used in two places: upstream for information extraction (proposing entities and relations checked against Wikidata-aligned schemas) and downstream embedded in a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) loop that generates answers conditioned on retrieved KG context. No specific LLM model name is given.
symbolic_component: Wikidata knowledge graph with its ontology (classes, properties via P31/P279), property constraints, EntitySchemas (ShEx in E namespace), SPARQL queries over the Wikidata Query Service (WDQS). Modelling patterns for persons, works, censorship decisions, repression cases, and provenance encoded as EntitySchemas.
symbolic_inference_evidence: The symbolic component operates at multiple pipeline phases: (1) during ontology alignment (Phase 2), EntitySchemas and property constraints validate candidate items against formal expectations before data enters the KG; (2) during reconciliation (Phase 3), OpenRefine's Wikidata reconciliation matches local rows to existing items using the KG; (3) at inference time in the RAG pipeline, SPARQL queries and graph-based expansion retrieve structured context (items, statements, references, EntitySchemas) from the KG, which is concatenated with the user query to constrain and ground LLM generation; (4) upstream, LLM-proposed entities/relations are checked against Wikidata-aligned schemas before being added to the KG. The KG ontology thus constrains both extraction and generation.
component_interaction: A three-phase ontology-first pipeline feeds curated data into a Wikidata-aligned KG (symbolic). Upstream, an LLM proposes entities/relations that are validated against EntitySchemas before entering the KG. Downstream, user queries trigger vector-based retrieval plus SPARQL/graph-based expansion over the KG; retrieved structured context is concatenated with the query and passed to the LLM for answer generation. The KG ontology constrains what the LLM can assert.
task: Data harmonization; Semantic annotation; Other: KG-grounded retrieval-augmented generation for humanities datasets
datasets: [{"name": "Cultura y censura (Francoist book-censorship files)", "size": "Tens of thousands of records; pilot: ~42,000 extracted fragments, ~11,000 relations", "domain": "Digital humanities / historical censorship"}, {"name": "SIDBRINT (Sistema d'Informació Digital sobre les Brigades Internacionals)", "size": "Not specified", "domain": "Digital humanities / Spanish Civil War"}]
metrics_best_result: Not specified (no quantitative evaluation reported; authors state full evaluation is future work)
baseline_gap: Not specified
explainability_mechanism: The ontology-backed KG provides structured, referenced constraints that underpin explainability: every statement carries provenance (archival references, sources), EntitySchemas serve as executable documentation of modelling expectations, SPARQL queries enable coverage and quality audits, and the RAG pipeline presents retrieved graph context (items, statements, references, EntitySchemas) as evidence alongside generated answers, encouraging attribution and flagging when information is insufficient.
explainability_evaluation: No formal evaluation of explainability is reported. The authors describe the design rationale and architecture but explicitly leave detailed evaluation of human-in-the-loop KG+RAG performance as future work.
hitl_details: Community-centred co-creation with historians, archivists, librarians, GLAM staff, community partners (including relatives of victims and community historians) involved in modelling decisions, curation sprints, and schema governance. The KG+RAG prototype is designed for curator validation of LLM-proposed statements, but the authors state they do not yet have robust statistics on acceptance/rejection rates. Detailed HITL evaluation is listed as future work.
D7_domain: other: digital humanities / historical censorship and repression
specific_sources: Wikidata KG (classes, properties, constraints, EntitySchemas, SPARQL endpoint), VIAF, GND, Getty authority files, library authority files, LLM (unspecified) for information extraction and RAG generation
frameworks_libraries: OpenRefine (Wikidata reconciliation), QuickStatements, Mix'n'match, Wikidata Query Service (SPARQL), ShEx (EntitySchemas)
main_contribution: Presents an ontology-first, community-centred neuro-symbolic workflow for integrating heterogeneous humanities datasets into Wikidata, combining EntitySchemas, SPARQL-based audits, and a KG-grounded RAG architecture to ensure explainability, provenance, and gender equity in the context of Francoist censorship and repression.
limitations_reported: Schemas must remain living documents as humanities concepts evolve; coverage does not guarantee equitable representation; CC0 licensing frictions with donor datasets; WDQS rate and complexity limits; no robust statistics yet on curator acceptance/rejection of LLM-proposed statements; full quantitative evaluation of KG+RAG performance is future work; hallucinations and bias within the KG itself must be confronted.
relevance_to_thesis: Reusable pipeline architecture; KG/ontology component; HITL mechanism; Multilingual case; Explainability mechanism; NeSy taxonomy/classification
free_notes: This is primarily a methodological/design paper and case study rather than an empirical evaluation. The KG+RAG layer is at prototype stage. The paper explicitly self-identifies as neuro-symbolic (HerStory NeSyAI project). Multilingual support covers Catalan, Spanish, Basque, and Galician via Wikidata's multilingual labels. The paper focuses on gender equity and visibility of women/minoritized genders. No quantitative matching or alignment metrics are reported.

authors: Teeradaj Racharak
title: On Approximation of Concept Similarity Measure in Description Logic ELH With Pre-Trained Word Embedding
neural_component: Pre-trained word embeddings (BioWordVec, trained with fastText on PubMed and MIMIC-III) used to compute cosine similarity between primitive concept names and role names in the ontology when exact matching fails.
symbolic_component: Description Logic ELH ontology (SNOMED CT); structural subsumption via homomorphism characterization on ELH description trees; TBox with concept definitions and role hierarchy axioms.
symbolic_inference_evidence: The symbolic component is the core of the entire inference pipeline. At inference time, concept descriptions are fully expanded using the TBox, transformed into ELH description trees, and the homomorphism degree (hdϵ) is computed structurally by traversing the trees (vertices and edges). The neural embeddings are only invoked when exact matching between primitive concept names or role names fails — cosine similarity from pre-trained embeddings approximates the matching degree. The backtrace table algorithm (Algorithm 1) extracts symbolic explanations as ELH concept descriptions representing shared sub-structures. Thus, the symbolic DL ontology structures and guides the similarity computation, while neural embeddings fill in when the symbolic matching is incomplete.
component_interaction: The DL ontology provides the structural framework: concepts are expanded into description trees, and a homomorphism degree is computed by traversing these trees. When primitive concept names or role names do not exactly match between two description trees, their corresponding pre-trained word embedding vectors are looked up via a mapping M, and cosine similarity is computed to approximate the matching degree. This neural-derived similarity score is propagated back into the symbolic homomorphism degree computation. The final similarity (simϵ) aggregates bidirectional homomorphism degrees. Explanations are extracted from the backtrace table as ELH concept descriptions (symbolic sub-structures).
task: Ontology alignment; Other: Concept similarity measure in DL ontologies
datasets: [{"name": "SNOMED CT (Clinical Finding and Procedure categories)", "size": "206 concepts sampled from Clinical Finding (C1) + 206 from Procedure (C2); full ontology has 379,691 concept names and 62 role names", "domain": "biomedical"}]
metrics_best_result: Concepts within the same category (C1×C1, C2×C2) are shown to be more similar than cross-category pairs (C1×C2), confirming the hypothesis. Execution time reported: avg 15.02ms for C1×C1, avg 13.56ms for C1×C2, avg 6.77ms for C2×C2. Qualitative comparison: simϵ(Mucus, AmnioticFluid) = 0.45 vs. cosine similarity on BioWordVec = 0.46, with added explainability.
baseline_gap: -0.01 (simϵ yields 0.45 with explainability)
explainability_mechanism: The system generates explanations as ELH concept descriptions representing shared sub-structures between two concepts. Algorithm 1 maintains a backtrace table during homomorphism degree computation, recording which primitives, existentials, and embeddings contributed to each degree. Two explanations are produced (one per direction of hdϵ). These can be translated into natural language using a template: vertices become 'it is [label]', edges become '[label] that allows to [role]', and successors become 'with [label]'. When embeddings are used, the explanation notes the similarity derived from the pre-trained embedding.
explainability_evaluation: Theoretical analysis (Proposition 2 guarantees two explanations always exist for any non-zero similarity); qualitative examples demonstrated (e.g., natural language explanations for NeonatalAspirationOfMucus vs NeonatalAspirationOfAmnioticFluid). No formal user study.
specific_sources: SNOMED CT (domain ontology, DL ELH), BioWordVec (pre-trained word embeddings trained with fastText on PubMed and MIMIC-III)
frameworks_libraries: Java 1.8, Spring Boot, OWL API
main_contribution: A neuro-symbolic framework (∼(T,E)) and concrete measure (simϵ) for explainable concept similarity in DL ELH ontologies, where pre-trained word embeddings approximate homomorphism degrees when exact symbolic matching fails, with a backtrace algorithm that extracts explanations as ELH concept descriptions in natural language.
limitations_reported: The measure simϵ is restricted to ontologies modeled in DL ELH (conjunction, existential restriction, top concept, concept definitions, role hierarchy); it does not cover ALC or DL-Lite family. The authors plan extensions to these logics as future work.
relevance_to_thesis: Reusable pipeline architecture; KG/ontology component; Explainability mechanism; NeSy taxonomy/classification; Transferable embedding component
free_notes: The author explicitly positions the work as 'inverse' neuro-symbolism: neural embeddings empower symbolic reasoning (rather than the more common direction of symbolic knowledge enhancing neural models). The framework is general and can be instantiated with different embeddings and ontologies. Theoretical soundness is proven through four desirable properties (symmetric, equivalent invariant, structurally dependent, preference invariant). Tables 2 and 3 provide detailed feature comparisons with logic-based and neuro-symbolic methods respectively.

authors: Watanee Jearanaiwongkul, Teeradaj Racharak
title: Human-Friendly Explanation for Ontology-based Concept Similarity: Design and Development
neural_component: Pre-trained word embeddings used within the ELH concept similarity measure (from Racharak 2021); LLMs (ChatGPT-4o and Gemini-1.5-flash) used for generating human-friendly natural language explanations from symbolic explanation structures.
symbolic_component: Description Logic ELH ontologies; knowledge graphs representing concept structures; homomorphism-based semantic similarity computation producing structured symbolic explanations (tuples with similarity scores, matched nodes, paths, and role comparisons).
symbolic_inference_evidence: The symbolic component operates at the core similarity computation phase: concept similarity in ELH ontologies is computed using homomorphism-based matching over description graphs, producing structured symbolic explanation tuples (node/path comparisons with scores). These symbolic outputs are then fed as input to LLMs which translate them into human-friendly natural language explanations. The symbolic reasoning thus generates the formal explanations that serve as the basis for the entire pipeline.
component_interaction: A prior neuro-symbolic framework (Racharak 2021) computes concept similarity in ELH ontologies using pre-trained word embeddings integrated with description logic reasoning, producing structured symbolic explanations. These symbolic explanation tuples are then passed to LLMs (ChatGPT-4o, Gemini-1.5-flash) via one-shot prompting to generate human-friendly natural language explanations in three formats (summary, detailed, table).
task: Ontology alignment; Other: Ontology-based concept similarity explanation generation
datasets: [{"name": "Curated dataset of 20 path-based explanations", "size": "20 explanation instances", "domain": "general (food, activities)"}]
metrics_best_result: Table Explanation same-path F1 = 0.947 (Gemini), Detailed Explanation same-path F1 = 0.919 (Gemini); ChatGPT-4o: Table same-path F1 = 0.857, Detailed same-path F1 = 0.824
baseline_score: F1 = 0.947 on Table Explanation same-path comparisons
baseline_gap: Not specified (comparison between two LLMs, not against a prior baseline)
explainability_mechanism: The system generates human-friendly natural language explanations from formal symbolic reasoning outputs. Two explanation types are defined: node-based (comparing similarity between two nodes) and path-based (comparing two paths in KGs). Path-based explanations are produced in three formats: summary, detailed, and table. LLMs are prompted to translate structured symbolic tuples into these readable formats.
explainability_evaluation: User study with 20 participants (10 per explanation type) recruited via Prolific, using 0–7 Likert scale ratings on understandability, readability, sufficiency, and interest in additional explanation. Additionally, quantitative evaluation of LLM-generated explanations using exact match, precision, recall, and F1 score.
hitl_details: User study with 20 participants providing feedback on explanation quality (8 questions per participant). Participants compensated via Prolific, diverse demographics (ages 19–65, multiple countries and education levels). Feedback was used to select path-based explanation format over node-based. No iterative HITL loop in the algorithmic process.
specific_sources: Description Logic ELH ontologies (knowledge graphs), pre-trained word embeddings (from Racharak 2021), ChatGPT-4o (OpenAI GPT-4o), Gemini-1.5-flash
frameworks_libraries: OpenAI API (GPT-4o), Gemini-1.5-flash
main_contribution: Investigates how LLMs can be prompted to transform formal symbolic explanations from a neuro-symbolic ontology concept similarity framework into human-friendly natural language explanations, evaluating two explanation designs (node-based and path-based) through user studies and quantitative metrics.
limitations_reported: LLMs tend to miss correct matches when description graphs increase in size (more nodes and deeper graphs); errors occur when edges are labeled by sets in explanation graphs; dataset is small (20 instances); only initial exploration with limited LLM models tested.
relevance_to_thesis: Explainability mechanism; KG/ontology component; HITL mechanism; NeSy taxonomy/classification
free_notes: This paper builds on Racharak (2021) which is the core neuro-symbolic similarity framework; this paper's primary contribution is the explainability layer that converts symbolic outputs to human-readable text via LLMs. The evaluation is preliminary with a very small dataset of 20 instances. Temperature was set to 0 for deterministic outputs.

authors: Zhiyuan Qi, Ziheng Zhang, Jiaoyan Chen, Xi Chen, Yuejia Xiang, Ningyu Zhang, Yefeng Zheng
title: Unsupervised Knowledge Graph Alignment by Probabilistic Reasoning and Semantic Embedding
source: Proceedings of the Thirtieth International Joint Conference on Artificial Intelligence (IJCAI-21)
neural_component: Semantic Embedding (SE) module compatible with various embedding-based KG alignment models including MTransE, IPTransE, GCNAlign, BootEA, IMUSE, MultiKE, and RDGCN. These models encode entities and relations into low-dimensional vector spaces using translational models (e.g., TransE) or graph neural networks (e.g., GCN). Entity embeddings and predicted entity mappings are produced via nearest neighbor search in the embedding space.
symbolic_component: Probabilistic Reasoning (PR) module based on the PARIS system, which performs probabilistic inference over relation functionality, inverse functionality, entity equivalence probabilities, and relation subsumption relationships using iterative probabilistic reasoning (Eqs. 2, 3, 5). It initializes mappings via lexical matching on attribute values (e.g., identical literals) and iteratively expands entity and relation mappings.
knowledge_representation: Other: Probabilistic alignment model over KG triples (relation triples and attribute triples) with probabilistic equivalence and subsumption relationships
symbolic_inference_evidence: The PR module operates at inference time throughout the iterative pipeline: (1) In the initial iteration (k=0), it computes relation functionality/inverse functionality and alternately estimates entity equivalence probabilities and relation subsumption using probabilistic reasoning (Eqs. 1-3). (2) In subsequent iterations (k>0), it is re-initialized with both its previous output and entity mappings/embeddings from the SE module (Eq. 4), then performs modified probabilistic reasoning that incorporates embedding similarity into entity equivalence estimation (Eq. 5, replacing Eq. 2). (3) In the final iteration (k=K), the PR module produces the final output entity mappings filtered by a probability threshold. The PR module also provides unaligned candidates to the SE module and generates alignment seeds from high-confidence mappings.
component_interaction: The PR module and SE module interact iteratively in a bidirectional loop: (1) The PR module first runs independently to produce initial entity mappings with probabilities. (2) High-confidence mappings are selected as alignment seeds to train the SE module. (3) The SE module predicts mappings on unaligned entities and produces entity embeddings. (4) Both entity mappings and embeddings from the SE module are fed back to the PR module, which re-initializes entity equivalence probabilities (Eq. 4) and incorporates embedding similarity into its probabilistic reasoning (Eq. 5). (5) This cycle repeats for K iterations, with the PR module producing the final output.
datasets: [{"name": "EN-FR-100K-V2 (OpenEA)", "size": "100,000 entities per KG", "domain": "general (DBpedia)"}, {"name": "EN-DE-100K-V2 (OpenEA)", "size": "100,000 entities per KG", "domain": "general (DBpedia)"}, {"name": "D-W-100K-V2 (OpenEA)", "size": "100,000 entities per KG", "domain": "general (DBpedia-Wikidata)"}, {"name": "D-Y-100K-V2 (OpenEA)", "size": "100,000 entities per KG", "domain": "general (DBpedia-YAGO)"}, {"name": "D-W-15K-V2 (OpenEA)", "size": "15,000 entities per KG", "domain": "general (DBpedia-Wikidata)"}, {"name": "MED-BBK-9K", "size": "9,162 entities per KG", "domain": "biomedical (medical KG and Baidu Baike)"}]
metrics_best_result: F1 = 0.996 on D-Y-100K (PRASE-MultiKE); F1 = 0.972 on EN-DE-100K (PRASE-MultiKE); F1 = 0.954 on EN-FR-100K (PRASE-BootEA/MultiKE); F1 = 0.923 on D-W-15K (PRASE-BootEA); F1 = 0.890 on D-W-100K (PRASE-BootEA); F1 = 0.711 on MED-BBK-9K (PRASE-MultiKE)
baseline_score: Average F1 across 6 datasets (PARIS is strongest conventional baseline, e.g., F1=0.926 on EN-FR-100K, 0.948 on EN-DE-100K, 0.854 on D-W-100K, 0.983 on D-Y-100K, 0.897 on D-W-15K, 0.499 on MED-BBK-9K)
baseline_gap: +5.96 pp average F1 over best conventional system; +28.6 pp average F1 over best embedding-based model
explainability_mechanism: The PR module outputs entity mappings with explicit equivalence probabilities (Po(e≡e')) and relation subsumption probabilities (P(r⊑r')), which are computed through interpretable probabilistic reasoning formulas (Eqs. 2, 3, 5). These probabilities provide a trace of the reasoning process and can indicate the confidence of each mapping.
explainability_evaluation: N/A
specific_sources: Knowledge graphs: DBpedia, YAGO, Wikidata, an authoritative medical KG, Baidu Baike KG. Embedding models: TransE, GCN-based embeddings (GCNAlign), BootEA, MultiKE, MTransE, IPTransE, IMUSE, RSN4EA, RDGCN. Lexical matching on entity attribute values (string matching, edit distance, word embedding similarity).
frameworks_libraries: Python (reimplementation of PARIS), OpenEA benchmark framework, NVIDIA Tesla M40 GPU
main_contribution: Proposes PRASE, an unsupervised iterative framework that integrates probabilistic reasoning (PARIS) and semantic embedding for knowledge graph entity alignment, achieving state-of-the-art performance without requiring alignment seeds.
limitations_reported: The framework converges quickly but embedding learning in each iteration is time-consuming; incorrect entity mappings from the SE module can negatively impact precision; performance depends on the quality of the chosen embedding-based model; future work needed to expand to other reasoning systems (e.g., LogMap) and inject prior knowledge from KG ontologies.
relevance_to_thesis: Reusable pipeline architecture; Transferable embedding component; KG/ontology component; Multilingual case; Reusable benchmark; NeSy taxonomy/classification; Evaluation metric/protocol
free_notes: PRASE is a standby case with rebuttal on file. The framework is modular: any embedding-based alignment model can serve as the SE module. Cross-lingual datasets (EN-FR, EN-DE) demonstrate multilingual capability. The probabilistic reasoning module (PARIS) provides logical consistency checking that embedding-based models lack. The iterative bidirectional interaction between probabilistic reasoning and neural embeddings is a clear example of Type 3 NeSy integration. MED-BBK-9K is a Chinese-language industry medical dataset.

authors: Yiwen Peng, Thomas Bonald, Fabian M. Suchanek
title: FLORA: Unsupervised Knowledge Graph Alignment by Fuzzy Logic
neural_component: Small language models used for computing string/literal similarity: LaBSE (multilingual sentence encoder) for cross-lingual datasets and PEARL (phrase embedding model) for monolingual datasets. These produce initial similarity scores between literals that are fed into the symbolic fuzzy logic system.
symbolic_component: Recursive Fuzzy Inference System (FIS) based on fuzzy logic rules that iteratively compute entity alignment and subrelation alignment scores. The system uses functionality and local functionality of relations, relation lists, min aggregation for entity alignment rules, harmonic mean for head-entity list matching, and α-mean (arithmetic mean × benefit-of-doubt constant α) for subrelation alignment. Convergence is proven via Knaster-Tarski fixed point theorem.
symbolic_inference_evidence: The fuzzy logic rules operate throughout the entire iterative alignment process at inference time. Entity alignment rules (Equation 1) combine structural signals (relation functionality, relation list matching) with literal similarities to compute entity matching scores. Subrelation alignment rules (Equation 2) aggregate entity-level co-occurrence evidence to derive relation subsumption/equivalence scores. These two rule sets alternate iteratively until convergence. The symbolic FIS is the core reasoning engine — the neural component only provides initial literal similarity scores as input variables to the FIS. Maximum assignment (Equation 3) enforces one-to-one entity matching as a post-processing constraint.
component_interaction: The neural language models (LaBSE or PEARL) compute pairwise string/literal similarities as a pre-processing step. These similarity scores are then used as fixed input variables (initial values) in the recursive Fuzzy Inference System. The FIS then iteratively applies fuzzy logic rules to propagate and refine entity and relation alignment scores through structural reasoning. The neural component does not receive feedback from the symbolic component — it operates once at initialization.
datasets: [{"name": "D-W-15K-V1 (OpenEA)", "size": "15K gold alignments", "domain": "general (DBpedia-Wikidata)"}, {"name": "D-W-15K-V2 (OpenEA)", "size": "15K gold alignments", "domain": "general (DBpedia-Wikidata)"}, {"name": "DBP15K ZH-EN", "size": "15K gold alignments", "domain": "general (DBpedia cross-lingual)"}, {"name": "DBP15K JA-EN", "size": "15K gold alignments", "domain": "general (DBpedia cross-lingual)"}, {"name": "DBP15K FR-EN", "size": "15K gold alignments", "domain": "general (DBpedia cross-lingual)"}, {"name": "OAEI KG Track memoryalpha-stexpanded (Mem-ST)", "size": "Not specified", "domain": "entertainment (Star Trek)"}, {"name": "OAEI KG Track starwars-swtor (Star-SWT)", "size": "Not specified", "domain": "entertainment (Star Wars)"}, {"name": "D-W-100K-V1 (OpenEA)", "size": "100K gold alignments", "domain": "general (DBpedia-Wikidata)"}, {"name": "D-W-100K-V2 (OpenEA)", "size": "100K gold alignments", "domain": "general (DBpedia-Wikidata)"}]
metrics_best_result: F1 = 0.975 (supervised) and F1 = 0.962 (unsupervised) on D-W-15K-V2; Overall F1 = 0.97 on Star-SWT; Overall F1 = 0.95 on Mem-ST; Hit@1 = 0.976 on DBP ZH-EN; F1 = 0.949 (unsupervised) on D-W-100K-V2
explainability_mechanism: For any pair of aligned entities, the fuzzy logic rule with the highest firing strength (Equation 1) provides a human-interpretable explanation. The explanation includes the specific relation alignments, functionality values, and entity alignments that contributed to the match. An example is given: Lady Gaga and Q19848 are aligned with score 0.984, explained by the alignment of birth dates, relation alignments (musicalArtist ≅ P175, birthDate⁻¹ ≅ P569⁻¹), and entity alignments (3-Way and Q659417).
explainability_evaluation: Qualitative example provided (Lady Gaga alignment); no formal user study or quantitative evaluation of explainability.
specific_sources: LaBSE (language-agnostic BERT sentence embeddings) for multilingual string similarity; PEARL (phrase representation model) for monolingual string similarity; fuzzy logic rules encoding structural knowledge (relation functionality, relation lists) derived from the input KGs themselves
frameworks_libraries: Not specified (CPU-based implementation; uses LaBSE and PEARL from HuggingFace)
main_contribution: FLORA is an unsupervised, interpretable knowledge graph alignment method based on a recursive Fuzzy Inference System that iteratively aligns both entities and relations, provably converges, handles dangling entities, and achieves state-of-the-art results on major EA and KG alignment benchmarks.
limitations_reported: Slower runtime on attribute-rich datasets (e.g., ~2 hours on DBP15K vs. minutes for lexical methods); structural matching may find structurally correct but semantically unintended matches (e.g., apprentice ↔ padawan); future work needed for more complex taxonomy structures (subclass reasoning, deductive closures).
relevance_to_thesis: Reusable pipeline architecture; Explainability mechanism; KG/ontology component; Multilingual case; Reusable benchmark; Evaluation metric/protocol; NeSy taxonomy/classification; Native explainability
free_notes: The system is primarily symbolic (fuzzy logic) with neural components limited to initialization of literal similarity scores. The fuzzy logic framework is general and could accommodate other aggregation functions. The convergence proof via Knaster-Tarski fixed point theorem is a notable theoretical contribution. Handles relation subsumption (⊆/⊇) in addition to equivalence. Languages covered: Chinese, Japanese, French, English. The benefit-of-doubt parameter α addresses the Open World Assumption (KG incompleteness).

authors: Jiaoyan Chen, Ernesto Jiménez-Ruiz, Ian Horrocks, Denvar Antonyrajah, Ali Hadian, Jaehun Lee
title: Augmenting Ontology Alignment by Semantic Embedding and Distant Supervision
neural_component: Siamese Neural Network (SiamNN) with MLP, BiRNN, or AttBiRNN sub-networks; OWL2Vec* ontology embeddings (optionally pre-trained with Word2Vec on Wikipedia corpus); contrastive loss training with Adam optimizer.
symbolic_component: LogMap (logic-based ontology matching system with lexical indexation, structural indexation, and logical reasoning); HermiT OWL 2 reasoner for computing class subsumers; manually defined class disjointness constraints (branch conflict rules); subsumption-based logical assessment for filtering predicted mappings.
symbolic_inference_evidence: At inference time, the symbolic components operate at multiple pipeline phases: (1) LogMap's anchor mappings and over-estimation mappings define the candidate space and seed mappings; (2) class disjointness constraints (defined over general ontology classes) filter false-positive anchor mappings to produce high-precision seed mappings for training; (3) the HermiT reasoner computes class subsumers used to check disjointness constraint violations; (4) after neural prediction, class disjointness constraints again filter predicted mappings; (5) subsumption-based logical assessment discards mappings where one class maps to multiple classes in a subclass relationship; (6) final ensemble merges filtered predictions with seed mappings.
component_interaction: LogMap (symbolic) first computes anchor mappings (high precision) and over-estimation mappings (high recall candidates). Class disjointness constraints plus HermiT reasoner filter anchors to produce seed mappings. Seeds serve as distant supervision to train a SiamNN using OWL2Vec* embeddings. The trained SiamNN scores candidate mappings from the over-estimation set. Post-prediction, symbolic disjointness constraints and subsumption-based logical assessment filter the neural predictions. Final output is the union of filtered predictions and seed mappings.
datasets: [{"name": "HeLiS-FoodOn", "size": "HeLiS: 277 classes + 20,318 instances (transformed to classes); FoodOn: 28,182 classes; 372 partial gold standard mappings", "domain": "Food / healthy lifestyles"}, {"name": "OAEI Conference Track (2020)", "size": "21 alignment tasks from 17 ontologies; 259 gold standard class mappings (ra1 reference)", "domain": "Conference organization"}]
metrics_best_result: F1≈ = 0.752 on HeLiS-FoodOn (LogMapanc-ML with ensemble); F1 = 0.778 on OAEI Conference Track (AMLoaei-ML)
baseline_score: F1≈ = 0.451 (AML on HeLiS-FoodOn); F1 = 0.781 (VeeAlign on OAEI Conference)
baseline_gap: +30.1 pp over AML on HeLiS-FoodOn; -0.3 pp vs VeeAlign on OAEI Conference (but AMLoaei-ML = 0.778)
explainability_mechanism: N/A
explainability_evaluation: N/A
specific_sources: OWL2Vec* (ontology-tailored language model), Word2Vec pre-trained on Wikipedia 2018, HeLiS ontology (OWL, DL ALCHIQ(D)), FoodOn ontology (OWL, DL SRIQ), LogMap lexical index, HermiT OWL 2 reasoner
frameworks_libraries: LogMap, AML v3.1, HermiT reasoner, OWL2Vec*, Word2Vec, Adam optimizer
main_contribution: A general ML extension to traditional ontology alignment systems that uses distant supervision from high-precision anchor mappings with class disjointness constraints, OWL2Vec* ontology embeddings, and Siamese Neural Networks to improve both precision and recall of ontology alignment.
limitations_reported: Performance depends on the precision of seed mappings; lower-precision seeds from full LogMap or AML outputs degrade results. Class disjointness constraints are manually defined (though semi-automatic derivation is suggested as future work). Path embedding did not improve over class embedding, possibly because OWL2Vec* already encodes hierarchical context. Exhaustive hyperparameter search would require significantly more computation. Improvements on the OAEI Conference track are smaller than on the industrial task due to smaller, less complex ontologies already well-optimized by existing systems.
relevance_to_thesis: Reusable pipeline architecture; Transferable embedding component; KG/ontology component; Reusable benchmark; Evaluation metric/protocol; NeSy taxonomy/classification
free_notes: The paper introduces an approximate precision/recall evaluation methodology for incomplete gold standards. The distant supervision strategy avoids the need for manually labeled training data by leveraging high-confidence mappings from existing systems. The extension is demonstrated as generalizable across multiple base systems (LogMap, AML). The class disjointness constraints act as symbolic knowledge injected into the training pipeline, making this a clear example of symbolic components guiding/constraining neural learning.

authors: Mingyu Jeon, Jaeyoung Suh, Suwan Cho
title: Schema Matching on Graph: Iterative Graph Exploration for Efficient and Explainable Data Integration
neural_component: GPT-4o-mini used as the core reasoning engine for topic entity extraction (LLM-based disambiguation), relation scoring, entity scoring, beam pruning, sufficiency verification, and final answer generation. BM25 and embedding-based (cosine similarity) scoring used in hybrid keyword generation and reranking stages.
symbolic_component: Wikidata Knowledge Graph accessed via iterative 1-hop SPARQL queries at a SPARQL endpoint. Template-based SPARQL queries retrieve relations and entities at each hop. Heuristic filtering excludes irrelevant metadata relations.
knowledge_representation: Other: Wikidata (RDF-based KG accessed via SPARQL endpoint and wbsearchentities API)
symbolic_inference_evidence: The symbolic component operates throughout the inference pipeline: (1) In TEE, Wikidata is queried via the wbsearchentities API to retrieve candidate entities (QIDs) with labels and descriptions; (2) In Graph Exploration, iterative 1-hop SPARQL queries retrieve adjacent relations and connected entities at each hop from the Wikidata SPARQL endpoint; (3) Heuristic filtering prunes irrelevant relations symbolically; (4) The accumulated knowledge triples (structured KG paths) are provided as explicit evidence to the LLM for final answer generation. The SPARQL query paths serve as human-verifiable reasoning chains.
component_interaction: The pipeline alternates between neural and symbolic components in an iterative loop: (1) TEE phase: hybrid BM25+embedding keyword generation → symbolic Wikidata API retrieval → hybrid reranking → LLM disambiguation selects a single Topic Entity (QID). (2) Graph Exploration phase: at each hop, SPARQL queries (symbolic) retrieve adjacent relations and entities from Wikidata, then the LLM (neural) scores relations and entities, performs beam pruning, and checks sufficiency. This loop repeats until sufficient information is gathered or max depth is reached. (3) Final Answer: all collected knowledge triples are provided to the LLM to derive the schema matching pair.
datasets: [{"name": "CMS (Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services) Test dataset from KG-RAG4SM repository", "size": "2,563 attribute pairs (25 positive, 2,538 negative)", "domain": "Medical / Electronic Health Records"}]
metrics_best_result: F1 = 41.67%, Precision = 43.48%, Recall = 40.00% on CMS dataset
explainability_mechanism: SMoG generates human-verifiable 1-hop SPARQL query exploration paths (reasoning chains) that trace the exact path through the Knowledge Graph from the topic entity to the final matching decision. Each step in the reasoning chain corresponds to a specific SPARQL query and its results, making the entire process transparent. The depth and content of reasoning chains are analyzed to understand decision-making behavior.
explainability_evaluation: Analytic study examining average depth of reasoning chains by prediction group (TP, TN, FP, FN) and TEE score analysis by prediction group. No formal user study was conducted; explainability is evaluated through analysis of intermediate outputs (reasoning chain depths, topic entity scores).
specific_sources: Wikidata (KG accessed via SPARQL endpoint and wbsearchentities API), GPT-4o-mini (pre-trained LLM), BM25 lexical scoring, embedding-based cosine similarity scoring
frameworks_libraries: GPT-4o-mini (OpenAI API), Wikidata SPARQL endpoint, Wikidata wbsearchentities API, BM25
main_contribution: Proposes SMoG, a framework that re-establishes query-based KG exploration for schema matching by adapting iterative 1-hop SPARQL queries (inspired by Think-on-Graph from KGQA), achieving competitive performance with explainable reasoning paths and without requiring massive vector indices.
limitations_reported: SMoG does not outperform the SOTA vector-based baseline KG-RAG4SM (F1 gap of ~6 pp); the framework lacks adaptive depth control to mitigate identified error modes (Over-thinking for FN, Premature Commitment for FP); evaluation is limited to a single dataset (CMS) with extreme class imbalance; no formal user study for explainability evaluation.
relevance_to_thesis: Reusable pipeline architecture; KG/ontology component; Explainability mechanism; Evaluation metric/protocol
free_notes: SMoG is a standby case with rebuttal on file. The framework does not surpass the SOTA baseline but demonstrates that query-based KG exploration is viable for schema matching. The analytic study on reasoning chain depths (Over-thinking vs. Premature Commitment) provides novel insights into LLM-KG reasoning dynamics. The paper uses Wikidata rather than domain-specific medical KGs like UMLS or SNOMED-CT. The dataset has extreme class imbalance (0.97% positive). The framework directly queries SPARQL endpoints, avoiding the need for KG embedding and vector index storage.

authors: Qiu Ji, Xiaoping Zhang, Yuxin Ye, Guilin Qi, Jiaye Li, Site Li, Jianjie Ren, Songtao Lu
title: Ontology Revision based on Pre-trained Language Models
neural_component: BERT pre-trained language model used to encode ontology axioms (translated to natural language via NaturalOWL) into dense vectors; cosine and Euclidean similarity metrics computed over these vectors to rank axioms.
symbolic_component: OWL/DL ontologies with TBox/ABox axioms; DL reasoner Pellet for detecting unsatisfiable concepts and checking coherence; computation of R-MUPS and R-MIPS (minimal unsatisfiability/incoherence-preserving subsets); integer linear programming (ILP) solver for computing minimal diagnoses; kernel revision operators and incision functions defined over Description Logic formalism.
symbolic_inference_evidence: The symbolic component operates throughout the pipeline: (1) Pellet reasoner identifies all unsatisfiable concepts in the merged ontology; (2) a black-box algorithm computes R-MUPS and R-MIPS (minimal incoherence-preserving subsets); (3) BERT-based scoring functions rank axioms within each R-MIPS by semantic similarity; (4) an ILP solver computes a minimal diagnosis (set of axioms to remove) over the extracted subsets; (5) coherence checking via the DL reasoner is used to minimize the diagnosis by removing redundant axioms. The symbolic reasoning (coherence checking, MIPS computation) thus constrains which axioms are candidates and validates the final revision solution.
component_interaction: Axioms from OWL ontologies are first translated into natural language sentences via NaturalOWL, then encoded into vectors by BERT (neural component). The similarity scores from BERT vectors are used by four scoring functions to rank axioms within symbolically-computed R-MIPS. The symbolic component (DL reasoner + ILP solver) uses these rankings to select axioms for removal and verifies coherence of the resulting ontology. The neural component feeds semantic similarity information into the symbolic revision process.
task: Ontology alignment; Other: Ontology revision / incoherence resolution
datasets: [{"name": "OAEI Conference Track (10 ontology pairs: OM0–OM9)", "size": "10 pairs, single ontologies 6–1024 axioms, pairs 399–1059 axioms, 5–84 unsatisfiable concepts", "domain": "conference organization"}, {"name": "km1500 (9 ontology pairs: KM0–KM8)", "size": "9 pairs, each pair 2000 axioms, 4–120 unsatisfiable concepts", "domain": "knowledge management (BT Digital Library)"}]
metrics_best_result: Evaluated by number of removed axioms (fewer is better) and computation time; reliableOnt_cos adapted algorithm removes fewest axioms (e.g., 15 for OM8) and saves up to ~90% computation time compared to all-R-MIPS baseline
baseline_score: Most efficient in time; removes minimal axioms w.r.t. all R-MIPS but may not be cardinality-minimal in adapted setting
baseline_gap: reliableOnt_cos removes fewer axioms in challenging cases (e.g., OM8: 15 vs. baseline failing at higher step lengths)
explainability_mechanism: The system computes MUPS (minimal unsatisfiability-preserving subsets) and MIPS (minimal incoherence-preserving subsets) which serve as explanations for why concepts are unsatisfiable. The scoring functions provide transparency into why specific axioms are selected for removal based on their semantic similarity scores to other axioms in the ontology.
explainability_evaluation: No formal user study or dedicated explainability evaluation; the MUPS/MIPS structures and axiom scores are analyzed quantitatively in experiments.
specific_sources: BERT (pre-trained language model for axiom encoding), OWL ontologies from OAEI Conference Track and km1500 benchmark, NaturalOWL (axiom-to-text translation tool)
frameworks_libraries: OWL API (Java), Pellet DL reasoner, BERT (Python), NaturalOWL, ILP solver
main_contribution: Proposes four BERT-based scoring functions for ranking ontology axioms during revision and two algorithms (all-R-MIPS and adapted group-by-group) that use pre-trained language model semantics to resolve incoherence when merging ontologies, achieving up to 90% time savings.
limitations_reported: Both algorithms may fail when an unsatisfiable concept has too many R-MUPS within limited time and memory; the adapted algorithm could not handle OM8 with step length 10; step length selection is ontology-dependent and requires tuning based on expressivity, number of unsatisfiable concepts, and ontology size.
relevance_to_thesis: Reusable pipeline architecture; Transferable embedding component; KG/ontology component; Explainability mechanism; Reusable benchmark; NeSy taxonomy/classification
free_notes: The paper addresses ontology revision (resolving incoherence when combining ontologies) rather than direct semantic matching, but the task is closely related to ontology alignment post-processing. The neural component (BERT) provides axiom semantics while the symbolic component (DL reasoning, MIPS computation, ILP) handles logical coherence. Four scoring functions and two algorithm variants are systematically compared across 19 ontology pairs with varying complexity.

authors: Yahia Atig, Ahmed Zahaf, Nadri Khiati
title: Impact of Agricultural Ontologies Evolution on the Alignment Preservative Adaptation
source: AGRIS on-line Papers in Economics and Informatics, Volume XVI, Number 3
neural_component: None. The approach is purely symbolic, relying on OWL-2 reasoning (Pellet reasoner) and belief revision operators for alignment adaptation.
symbolic_component: OWL-2 ontologies, alignment correspondences, conservativity principle formalized via logical consequence, MCUR (Minimal Conservative Upon Revision) computation, kernel contraction operators inspired by belief revision theory, Pellet reasoner for inference.
symbolic_inference_evidence: The symbolic component operates throughout the entire pipeline: (1) detection phase uses OWL-2 reasoning (Pellet) to check whether the image of a changed axiom is a logical consequence of the ontology in isolation, identifying conservativity violations; (2) repair phase uses MCUR algorithm and hitting set algorithm to compute minimal subsets of correspondences responsible for violations and applies incision functions to remove them.
integration_mode: Other: Purely symbolic pipeline (no neural component)
component_interaction: No neural component is present. The pipeline is entirely symbolic: ontological changes are identified between ontology versions, detection patterns are applied using OWL-2 reasoning to find conservativity violations, and a repair process based on kernel contraction removes minimal sets of correspondences to restore conservativity.
datasets: [{"name": "Agronomy Ontology (AgrO) v1 and v5", "size": "4163 classes, 209 properties, 552 individuals (v5)", "domain": "agricultural"}, {"name": "Plant Trait Ontology (TO)", "size": "5262 classes, 159 properties", "domain": "agricultural"}, {"name": "Agricultural and Nutrition Technology Ontology (ANT)", "size": "127 classes, 4 properties, 4 individuals", "domain": "agricultural"}]
metrics_best_result: AgrO-1↔TO↔AgrO-5: 2 violations detected out of 596 correspondences, repair rate 0.33%, time 10.5 ns; AgrO-1↔ANT↔AgrO-5: 1 violation out of 10 correspondences, repair rate 10%, time 0.59 ns
baseline_gap: Not specified
explainability_mechanism: The MCUR computation identifies the minimal subset of correspondences responsible for each conservativity violation, and the hitting set tree structure traces the reasoning path showing which correspondences cause violations and which incision functions resolve them.
explainability_evaluation: N/A
specific_sources: Agronomy Ontology (AgrO), Plant Trait Ontology (TO), Agricultural and Nutrition Technology Ontology (ANT), AgroPortal repository, UMLS (mentioned as example)
frameworks_libraries: Java, OWL API, Align API, Pellet reasoner
main_contribution: Proposes a set of 24 detection and repair patterns for adapting ontology alignments to preserve conservativity when OWL-2 ontologies evolve, validated on agricultural ontologies.
limitations_reported: Only equivalence relations in alignment correspondences are considered; no reference alignments exist for measuring accuracy w.r.t. conservativity; the approach needs further study on the impact of repairs on alignment interoperability; a threshold between adaptation and recalculation from scratch needs to be established.
relevance_to_thesis: KG/ontology component; Evaluation metric/protocol
free_notes: This is a purely symbolic approach with no neural component. It addresses ontology alignment evolution and conservativity preservation rather than semantic matching per se. The work is entirely based on logical reasoning (OWL-2 + Pellet) and belief revision theory. Agricultural domain ontologies from AgroPortal are used for validation.

authors: Weizhuo Li, Qiu Ji, Songmao Zhang, Xuefeng Fu, Guilin Qi
title: A graph-based method for interactive mapping revision in DL-Lite
neural_component: Not specified. The system does not employ a neural component; it is a purely symbolic graph-based reasoning method with human-in-the-loop interactive decisions.
symbolic_component: DL-Lite ontologies encoded as integrated directed graphs; graph-based decision space with entail and conflict binary relations; transformational rules (R1–R12) converting ontology axioms and mappings into graph arcs; influence relations for enhanced incoherence detection; soft principles (SP1 coherence, SP2 matching support) for identifying reliable mappings.
symbolic_inference_evidence: The symbolic component operates throughout the entire inference pipeline: (1) at initialization, DL-Lite ontologies and mappings are transformed into an integrated graph via 12 transformational rules; (2) at each interactive step, the graph-based decision space computes entail (▷) and conflict (◁) relations to automatically propagate expert approve/decline decisions to other mapping arcs in polynomial time; (3) the weighted graph-based impact function ranks mappings by influence to select the most impactful mapping for the next expert decision; (4) Algorithm 6 (CalculateMIPS) detects incoherence via path-unsatisfiability in the enhanced integrated graph with influence arcs; (5) soft principles filter reliable mappings before interaction to reduce expert burden.
integration_mode: Other: Symbolic reasoning with human-in-the-loop (no neural component)
component_interaction: The system is entirely symbolic: DL-Lite ontologies and mappings are encoded into an integrated directed graph. A graph-based decision space propagates expert decisions (approve/decline) via entail and conflict relations, automatically labeling additional mappings. An impact function based on graph structure and mapping weights selects the most influential mapping to present to the expert in each interaction round. No neural component is present; integration is between symbolic graph-based reasoning and human expert decisions.
datasets: [{"name": "I3CON (WineA-WineB)", "size": "33–63 concepts", "domain": "wine"}, {"name": "OAEI Benchmark Track (BibOnto101-301, BibOnto101-302)", "size": "15–37 concepts", "domain": "bibliography"}, {"name": "OAEI Conference Track (24 ontology pairs from 7 ontologies)", "size": "30–141 concepts per ontology", "domain": "conference management"}, {"name": "OAEI Anatomy Track (mouse-human)", "size": "2744 and 3304 concepts", "domain": "biomedical anatomy"}, {"name": "OAEI Large BioMed Track FMA-NCI-small", "size": "3696 and 6488 concepts", "domain": "biomedical"}, {"name": "OAEI Large BioMed Track FMA-NCI-whole", "size": "78989 and 66724 concepts", "domain": "biomedical"}]
metrics_best_result: Graph-Repair⋆ achieves 19% average improvement in saving ratio over baselines; F1' = 0.951 on FMA-NCI-small by FCA-Map (Oracle view); SavingRatio up to 0.280 on Conference-all by HMatch (interactive evaluation)
baseline_gap: Graph-Repair⋆ achieves higher SavingRatio (0.280 vs 0.222) but slightly lower F1'_Macro (0.590 vs 0.600)
explainability_mechanism: The integrated graph provides visual and structural explanations: clash paths in the directed graph directly show the cause of incoherence (path-unsatisfiable nodes reaching both S and ¬S). The graph-based decision space makes entailment and conflict relations between mappings transparent. A user interface displays contextual information, mapping impacts, and graph structure to experts for decision-making.
explainability_evaluation: Evaluated via interactive experiments with 3 volunteer postgraduate experts on 6 alignment tasks; oracle positive precision and oracle negative precision metrics measure expert decision quality; comparison of 6 revision systems across saving ratio and F1 measures.
hitl_details: 3 postgraduate volunteers familiar with OWL and description logics serve as experts. In each interaction round, the system presents the most influential mapping (ranked by weighted graph-based impact function) to the expert who makes an approve/decline decision. The graph-based decision space then automatically propagates this decision to other mappings via entail/conflict relations, reducing total manual decisions. SavingRatio ranges from 2.3% to 28.0% depending on alignment. Soft principles for 'reliable' mappings further reduce decisions by 9.4%–36.2% for large-scale ontologies.
specific_sources: FMA (Foundational Model of Anatomy), NCI Thesaurus, OAEI Conference Track ontologies (cmt, Conference, confOf, edas, ekaw, iasted, sigkdd), OAEI Anatomy Track ontologies (mouse, human), I3CON Wine ontologies, OAEI Benchmark Track ontologies
frameworks_libraries: Java 1.8, OWLAPI, Neo4j
main_contribution: A polynomial-time graph-based method for interactive mapping revision in DL-Lite that encodes ontologies and mappings into an integrated directed graph, uses a graph-based decision space to automatically propagate expert decisions, and employs a weighted impact function to minimize manual effort, achieving 19% average improvement in saving ratio.
limitations_reported: Limited to DL-Lite ontologies requiring approximation for more expressive DL languages; cyclic integrated graphs reduce algorithm performance; dynamic ontology updates require incremental repair methods not yet developed; removal-only strategy may discard correct mappings when inherent incompatibilities exist between ontologies; i.i.d. assumption may not hold for large-scale knowledge bases.
relevance_to_thesis: Reusable pipeline architecture; KG/ontology component; HITL mechanism; Explainability mechanism; Reusable benchmark; Evaluation metric/protocol
free_notes: This is a purely symbolic system with no neural component. The NeSy aspect is limited to the combination of formal symbolic reasoning (graph-based DL-Lite inference) with human expert decisions in the loop. The paper extensively evaluates interactive vs. automatic mapping revision across 16 alignments from OAEI benchmarks. The enhanced integrated graph with influence relations extends the method beyond strict DL-Lite to handle qualified existential restrictions.

authors: Xiaomin Zhu, Bo Liu, Jinning Li, Xuebin Zhuang
title: LEAF: Logic-Enhanced Adaptive Fusion for Large-Scale Ontology Alignment
source: 2025 IEEE Cyber Science and Technology Congress (CyberSciTech)
neural_component: Two MLP classifiers (4D and 6D models): the 4D model operates on 1536-dimensional feature vectors from all-MiniLM-L6-v2 sentence embeddings (384-dim); the 6D model uses a 6-dimensional interpretable feature vector. A 4-bit quantized Qwen2.5-7B LLM generates Genus-Differentia logical decompositions. A Cross-Encoder model scores genus/differentia similarity and holistic concept similarity.
symbolic_component: Ontology hierarchical structure (subClassOf relations) used for topology fingerprints via GCN; Genus-Differentia logical decomposition pattern from description logic tradition; one-to-one alignment constraint enforced as structural prior; progressive recall strategy uses lexical indexing and structural seed propagation from prior iteration alignments; conflict resolution via greedy selection.
symbolic_inference_evidence: At inference time, the symbolic components operate at multiple pipeline phases: (1) Stage 1 (Search Space Pruning) uses lexical matching, semantic thresholding, and structural seed generation from previous iteration alignments (exploiting ontology hierarchical relations) to construct the candidate set; (2) Stage 2 (Likelihood Estimation) computes a semantically-decoupled topology fingerprint from graph-theoretic metrics (in-degree, betweenness centrality) of the ontology structure, propagated through a fixed-weight GCN—this is a purely structural/symbolic signal; LLM-generated Genus-Differentia decompositions provide logic-based features (flogic_g, flogic_d) as input to the 6D model; (3) Stage 3 (Evidence Fusion) applies the one-to-one alignment constraint as a structural prior during conflict resolution via greedy selection.
component_interaction: The pipeline proceeds in three stages. First, symbolic operations (lexical indexing, semantic thresholding, structural seed propagation from ontology hierarchy) prune the search space to generate candidates. Second, two parallel neural matchers estimate match likelihood: the 4D MLP uses purely neural sentence embeddings, while the 6D MLP combines neural features (semantic similarity, cross-encoder scores) with symbolic/structural features (topology fingerprint from ontology graph, LLM-generated genus-differentia logical features). Third, a two-level adaptive fusion mechanism combines the two matcher scores using validation-set-derived weights and consistency calibration. Finally, symbolic constraints (one-to-one matching, thresholding, greedy conflict resolution) produce the final alignment. The process iterates, with alignments from one iteration feeding structural priors into the next iteration's recall stage.
datasets: [{"name": "OAEI Anatomy track", "size": "Source: 2,744 concepts (AMA), Target: 3,304 concepts (NCI Thesaurus); 1,516 reference correspondences; 40% hold-out test set (538 alignments)", "domain": "biomedical"}]
metrics_best_result: F1 = 0.883 (P = 0.905, R = 0.863) on OAEI Anatomy hold-out test set
explainability_mechanism: The 6D model is designed as an interpretable 'glass-box' model using 6 human-understandable features (semantic similarity, topology fingerprint, genus similarity, differentia similarity, holistic cross-encoder similarity, disambiguation score). Each feature has a clear semantic meaning, enabling feature-level interpretation of match decisions. The authors describe this as 'interpretable by design.' The LLM-generated Genus-Differentia decompositions provide structured, human-readable logical justifications for semantic relatedness.
explainability_evaluation: Ablation study demonstrates contribution of individual components (4D-only vs 6D-only vs full system). No dedicated user study or formal explainability evaluation is reported. The authors note that providing feature attribution analyses for the 6D model's predictions remains a promising future direction.
specific_sources: all-MiniLM-L6-v2 (Sentence-Transformers, pre-trained neural embeddings), Qwen2.5-7B (4-bit quantized LLM for Genus-Differentia feature generation), Cross-Encoder model, ontology hierarchical structure (subClassOf relations from AMA and NCI Thesaurus)
frameworks_libraries: Python 3.9, PyTorch 2.1, Sentence-Transformers, NetworkX, PyTorch Geometric, Qwen2.5-7B (4-bit quantized)
main_contribution: LEAF is an iterative ontology alignment framework that combines progressive candidate recall, complementary heterogeneous matchers (a neural 4D model and an interpretable 6D model enriched with topology fingerprints and LLM-generated Genus-Differentia logical features), and a two-level adaptive fusion mechanism to achieve competitive F1 on the OAEI Anatomy benchmark.
limitations_reported: LLM-generated logical features may introduce noise (imperfect Genus-Differentia decompositions); cross-species semantic shift phenomenon affects logical feature quality; the framework has been evaluated only on the biomedical anatomy domain; feature attribution analysis for the 6D model is identified as future work; the system uses a portion of the reference alignment for training (60/40 split), unlike some baselines evaluated on the full reference.
relevance_to_thesis: Reusable pipeline architecture; Transferable embedding component; KG/ontology component; Explainability mechanism; Evaluation metric/protocol; NeSy taxonomy/classification
free_notes: LEAF uses LLMs not as end-to-end deciders but as feature engineers (generative-discriminative paradigm). The 6D model's interpretable features and the topology fingerprint (semantically-decoupled structural signal) are notable design choices. The comparison with OAEI baselines is somewhat complicated by the fact that LEAF reports on a 40% hold-out test split while OAEI systems are evaluated on the full reference alignment. The paper explicitly frames ontology alignment as relevant to indicator system fusion.

authors: Jiaoyan Chen, Pan Hu, Ernesto Jimenez-Ruiz, Ole Magnus Holter, Denvar Antonyrajah, Ian Horrocks
title: OWL2Vec*: Embedding of OWL Ontologies
source: arXiv preprint arXiv:2009.14654v2 (Machine Learning, Springer journal submission)
