neural_component: Word2Vec skip-gram model trained on ontology-derived corpus; optional pre-training on English Wikipedia dump; downstream classifiers include Random Forest, MLP, SVC, Logistic Regression
symbolic_component: OWL ontologies (SROIQ DL expressivity); OWL-to-RDF graph transformation via W3C OWL-to-RDF Graph Mapping and projection rules; HermiT OWL 2 reasoner for TBox classification and ABox realization; Manchester Syntax axiom serialization; Weisfeiler-Lehman sub-graph kernels
symbolic_inference_evidence: The OWL ontology is processed symbolically before embedding: (1) the HermiT reasoner optionally computes entailment (TBox classification, ABox realization) to materialize inferred axioms; (2) the ontology is transformed into an RDF graph via W3C mapping or projection rules that approximate complex logical constructors; (3) axioms are serialized into Manchester Syntax sentences capturing logical constructors (e.g., existential/universal quantification, disjointness); (4) random walks with optional WL sub-graph kernels are computed over the RDF graph. All these symbolic steps produce the corpus that is then fed into the neural Word2Vec model for embedding learning.
component_interaction: Symbolic-first pipeline: The OWL ontology is first optionally reasoned over by HermiT, then transformed into an RDF graph (via W3C mapping or projection rules). Random walks and Manchester Syntax axiom sentences are extracted from the graph to produce a structure document. Lexical and combined documents are generated by replacing IRIs with labels/annotations. These three documents form the corpus for training a Word2Vec skip-gram model. The resulting entity and word embeddings are concatenated and fed to a downstream classifier (RF, MLP) for ontology completion tasks.
task: Ontology alignment; Other: Ontology completion (class membership prediction and class subsumption prediction)
datasets: [{"name": "HeLis", "size": "20,318 instances, 277 classes, 172,213 axioms", "domain": "healthy lifestyle / food"}, {"name": "FoodOn", "size": "359 instances, 28,182 classes, 241,581 axioms", "domain": "food"}, {"name": "Gene Ontology (GO)", "size": "44,244 classes, 513,306 axioms", "domain": "bioinformatics / gene"}]
metrics_best_result: MRR = 0.953, Hits@1 = 0.932 on HeLis (membership prediction); MRR = 0.213, Hits@1 = 0.143 on FoodOn (subsumption prediction); MRR = 0.170, Hits@1 = 0.076 on GO (subsumption prediction)
baseline_gap: +5.4 pp on HeLis, +7.7 pp on FoodOn, +4.7 pp on GO (MRR)
explainability_mechanism: t-SNE visualization of learned embeddings and analysis of Euclidean distances between entity pairs (positive vs. negative axioms) to interpret and validate the discriminative quality of the embeddings.
explainability_evaluation: Qualitative visualization analysis and quantitative Euclidean distance ratios between positive and negative samples; no formal user study.
specific_sources: OWL ontologies (HeLis, FoodOn, Gene Ontology) as domain ontology/KG; Word2Vec pre-trained on English Wikipedia dump as pre-trained neural resource; HermiT OWL 2 reasoner
frameworks_libraries: scikit-learn, pyRDF2Vec, OpenKE, TensorFlow, Word2Vec, HermiT reasoner
main_contribution: OWL2Vec* is an ontology embedding framework that jointly encodes the graph structure, logical constructors, and lexical information of OWL ontologies via random walks and word embeddings, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art KG and ontology embedding methods on class membership and subsumption prediction tasks.
limitations_reported: Pre-training on Wikipedia does not improve and can slightly harm performance; the combined document can introduce noise to word embeddings; IRI embeddings concatenated with word embeddings sometimes hurt subsumption prediction; zero-shot learning scenarios (new classes without known instances) dramatically reduce performance (Hits@1 drops to 65.6%); simple concatenation of IRI and word vectors without a more sophisticated integration mechanism; Word2Vec is used but contextual models like BERT are left for future work.
relevance_to_thesis: Reusable pipeline architecture; Transferable embedding component; KG/ontology component; Reusable benchmark; NeSy taxonomy/classification
free_notes: The paper explicitly positions itself as bridging symbolic and sub-symbolic knowledge representation. Ontology alignment is mentioned as an ongoing application (reference [10]) using OWL2Vec* embeddings. The framework is modular: the word embedding model (Word2Vec) can be replaced by other sequence learning methods. Extensive ablation covers document types, graph transformation strategies, walking depth, WL kernels, reasoning, pre-training, and classifiers.

authors: Jaydeep Chakraborty, Srividya K. Bansal, Luca Virgili, Krishanu Konar, Beyza Yaman
title: OntoConnect: Unsupervised Ontology Alignment with Recursive Neural Network
source: SAC '21: Proceedings of the 36th Annual ACM Symposium on Applied Computing
neural_component: Recursive neural network using dynamic-array tree-LSTM cells; fastText pre-trained word embeddings (100d, 200d, 300d) trained on Wikipedia 2017, UMBC WebBase corpus, and statmt.org news dataset; unsupervised learning where the model takes meta-information of ontological classes as input and predicts the class vector
symbolic_component: OWL ontologies parsed using OWL API and HermiT Reasoner to extract structural meta-information (IRI, label, restrictions, parent classes, child classes, equivalent classes, disjoint classes) for each ontological class/concept
symbolic_inference_evidence: At inference time, the HermiT reasoner and OWL API are used to parse both source and target ontologies to extract structural meta-information (parent, child, equivalent, disjoint, restriction classes) for each concept. This extracted symbolic structure is then fed as input to the recursive neural network for prediction. The meta-similarity computed by the neural model is combined with word similarity via weighted harmonic mean, and a similarity threshold filters the final alignment candidates.
component_interaction: First, the symbolic component (OWL API + HermiT Reasoner) parses ontologies and extracts structural meta-information for each class. These symbolic features are converted to vectors using fastText embeddings. The recursive tree-LSTM neural network is trained unsupervised on source ontology meta-information. At prediction, meta-information of target classes is fed to the trained model to produce predicted vectors. Word similarity (cosine between class label vectors) and meta similarity (cosine between predicted and source vectors) are combined via weighted harmonic mean. A similarity threshold filters final alignments.
datasets: [{"name": "OAEI Anatomy dataset", "size": "Source: 3304 classes (NCI Thesaurus), Target: 2744 classes (Adult Mouse Anatomy), 1497 reference alignments", "domain": "biomedical"}]
metrics_best_result: Precision = 97.71%, Recall = 68.27%, F-measure = 80.38% on OAEI Anatomy (300d, threshold 0.96, top-1); Best F-measure = 80.74% (300d, threshold 0.92, top-3)
baseline_gap: Not specified
explainability_mechanism: The system provides separate word similarity and meta similarity scores for each alignment candidate, along with their combined similarity score. The case study (Table 2) shows rankings of actual source classes by word similarity, meta similarity, and combined similarity, allowing users to trace which similarity component contributed to or detracted from a correct prediction.
explainability_evaluation: Qualitative case study analysis of 14 test cases examining how word similarity and meta similarity rankings interact to produce correct or incorrect predictions
specific_sources: fastText pre-trained embeddings (trained on Wikipedia 2017, UMBC WebBase corpus, statmt.org news), OWL ontologies (NCI Thesaurus, Adult Mouse Anatomy), HermiT OWL Reasoner for structural inference
frameworks_libraries: OWL API, HermiT Reasoner, fastText, LSTM (recursive neural network), Google Cloud Platform
main_contribution: Proposes OntoConnect, an unsupervised domain-independent ontology alignment system that uses a recursive tree-LSTM neural network trained on structural meta-information extracted from OWL ontologies combined with fastText word embeddings to align ontological classes without human intervention or domain knowledge.
limitations_reported: The system was only tested on the OAEI Anatomy dataset; recall is relatively low compared to precision; the approach has not yet been evaluated in the full OAEI competition environment or on other datasets such as SWEET or Large Biomedical Ontologies; the user interface for analyzing alignments is planned for future work.
relevance_to_thesis: Reusable pipeline architecture; Transferable embedding component; KG/ontology component; Reusable benchmark; Evaluation metric/protocol
free_notes: The system combines syntactic (word) similarity and structural (meta) similarity via weighted harmonic mean. The ablation-like analysis varies similarity threshold (0.90-0.99), vector dimension (100d, 200d, 300d), and top-k predictions (k=1,3,5). No direct numerical comparison with OAEI participating systems is provided, though the authors claim performance close to domain-specific approaches. The system design explicitly aims to eliminate the need for domain expertise and human intervention.

authors: Shengyuan Chen, Zheng Yuan, Qinggang Zhang, Wen Hua, Jiannong Cao, Xiao Huang
title: NeuSymEA: Neuro-symbolic Entity Alignment via Variational Inference
neural_component: Graph neural network (GCN-based or translation-based models such as Dual-AMN and LightEA) that parameterizes the variational distribution qθ over entity alignment truth scores; learns entity embeddings to compute pairwise matching scores.
symbolic_component: Weighted cross-KG logic rules mined from relational paths connecting entity pairs across two KGs; rules are formulated in a Markov random field (MRF) that models the joint probability of all alignment truth scores; sub-relation probabilities psub(r⊆r') and relation functionality η(r) serve as learned rule weights.
symbolic_inference_evidence: During inference, the symbolic component operates at multiple phases: (1) In the E-step, weighted logic rules compute pw(v(e,e')|vO) to predict alignment probabilities for hidden pairs, which are then used as pseudo-labels (positive if above threshold δ) to train the neural model. (2) In the M-step, the neural model's predictions update rule weights via Equation 10. (3) At final inference, the symbolic model infers alignment probabilities with optimized weights w, and an explainer generates supporting rule paths with confidence scores via breadth-first search and rule decomposition reversal. The symbolic rules also filter candidates through lazy inference and one-to-one matching constraints.
component_interaction: NeuSymEA models the joint probability of truth scores in a Markov random field regulated by weighted logic rules. Optimization uses variational EM: in the E-step, the symbolic model (with fixed rule weights w) produces alignment probabilities used as pseudo-labels to train the neural model (updating θ); in the M-step, the neural model (with fixed θ) produces pseudo-labels that update the rule weights w. This bidirectional alternation continues iteratively until convergence. At inference, both components jointly predict alignments, and an explainer reverses rule decomposition to provide interpretable supporting paths.
datasets: [{"name": "DBP15K (full version)", "size": "~66k–106k entities per KG, 15,000 aligned pairs per language pair", "domain": "general (DBpedia cross-lingual)"}, {"name": "DBP15K (condensed version)", "size": "~19k–20k entities per KG, 15,000 aligned pairs per language pair", "domain": "general (DBpedia cross-lingual)"}, {"name": "OpenEA100K", "size": "100K entities", "domain": "general"}, {"name": "DBP1M", "size": "1M entities", "domain": "general"}]
metrics_best_result: Hit@1 = 0.858 on condensed DBP15K FR-EN (NeuSymEA-L); Hit@1 = 0.834 on full DBP15K FR-EN (NeuSymEA-L); MRR = 0.894 on condensed DBP15K FR-EN (NeuSymEA-L)
explainability_mechanism: An explainer module generates supporting rule paths for any queried entity pair. It operates in two modes: (1) hard-anchor mode using only prealigned pairs as anchors, and (2) soft-anchor mode using both prealigned and inferred pairs. For each pair, the explainer performs breadth-first search to find reachable anchor pairs, extracts relational paths, and computes rule confidence scores as the product of sub-relation probabilities and relation functionality scores. Rules are ranked by confidence, providing explicit evidence for alignment or misalignment.
explainability_evaluation: Qualitative evaluation via visualization of confidence distributions for positive vs. negative pairs (Figure 4); analysis of number of supporting rules as a function of maximum rule length; case study examples of supporting rules (Table 2). No formal user study.
specific_sources: DBpedia (DBP15K cross-lingual KG pairs: ZH-EN, JA-EN, FR-EN), OpenEA, DBP1M
frameworks_libraries: Not specified (mentions GPU: NVIDIA RTX 3090, parallel/batch processing)
main_contribution: A unified neuro-symbolic framework for entity alignment that models the joint probability of alignment truth scores in a Markov random field with weighted cross-KG rules, optimized via variational EM with logical deduction for efficient long-rule reasoning and an explainer providing interpretable supporting paths with confidence scores.
limitations_reported: Currently designed for pairwise EA between two KGs; extending to simultaneous multi-KG alignment may require inefficient iterative pairwise alignments and a more sophisticated optimization paradigm.
relevance_to_thesis: Reusable pipeline architecture; KG/ontology component; Explainability mechanism; Multilingual case; Reusable benchmark; Evaluation metric/protocol; NeSy taxonomy/classification
free_notes: NeuSymEA achieves 7.6% Hit@1 improvement on DBP15K ZH-EN over strong baselines. Demonstrates remarkable low-resource robustness (73.7% Hit@1 on FR-EN with only 1% seed alignments). The variational EM framework provides principled bidirectional integration of symbolic rules and neural embeddings. The explainer with hard/soft anchor modes is a distinctive contribution for interpretability in entity alignment. Uses Dual-AMN and LightEA as interchangeable neural backbones (NeuSymEA-D and NeuSymEA-L variants).

authors: Jiang, T., Bu, C., Zhu, Y., Wu, X.
title: Integrating Symbol Similarities with Knowledge Graph Embedding for Entity Alignment: An Unsupervised Framework
neural_component: Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) for learning structural embeddings of entities; fastText word embeddings for computing semantic similarity of entity names
symbolic_component: String similarity (Levenshtein-based analogy editing distance), path similarity with parameter swapping strategy over KG triples, collective similarity construction integrating multiple symbolic similarity measures
symbolic_inference_evidence: At inference time, symbolic similarity measures (string similarity, semantic similarity, path similarity with parameter swapping) are computed to construct a collective similarity matrix Sc. This matrix generates high-quality initial alignment seeds A0 by filtering entity pairs via a high threshold (β=0.95) with bidirectional matching constraints. The collective similarity Sc is then combined with the GCN-learned structural similarity Se in the iterative alignment loop (S = w4*Se + (1-w4)*Sc), guiding entity pair selection at each iteration. The symbolic path similarity with parameter swapping also iteratively propagates alignment decisions through KG triples.
component_interaction: Phase 1 (symbolic): Multiple symbolic similarity features (string, semantic via fastText, path with parameter swapping) are integrated into a collective similarity matrix Sc, which generates initial alignment seeds A0. Phase 2 (neural+symbolic iterative): A0 is fed to a GCN that learns structural embeddings and computes structural similarity Se. Se is combined with Sc into a final similarity matrix S. New aligned entity pairs are generated from S and fed back to retrain the GCN iteratively until convergence. The symbolic component thus guides and constrains the neural component throughout the pipeline.
datasets: [{"name": "DBP_ZH-EN", "size": ">15k entities", "domain": "general (DBpedia cross-lingual)"}, {"name": "DBP_JA-EN", "size": ">15k entities", "domain": "general (DBpedia cross-lingual)"}, {"name": "DBP_FR-EN", "size": ">15k entities", "domain": "general (DBpedia cross-lingual)"}, {"name": "DBP-YG", "size": ">15k entities", "domain": "general (DBpedia-YAGO3)"}]
metrics_best_result: F1 = 0.960 on DBP_FR-EN; F1 = 0.959 on DBP_JA-EN; F1 = 0.945 on DBP_ZH-EN
baseline_score: F1 = 0.951 on DBP_FR-EN; F1 = 0.939 on DBP_JA-EN; F1 = 0.906 on DBP_ZH-EN
baseline_gap: +0.9 pp on DBP_FR-EN; +2.0 pp on DBP_JA-EN; +3.9 pp on DBP_ZH-EN
explainability_mechanism: N/A
explainability_evaluation: N/A
specific_sources: DBpedia, YAGO3 (domain KGs used as input); fastText pre-trained word embeddings for semantic similarity
frameworks_libraries: Not specified
main_contribution: An unsupervised entity alignment framework (SE-UEA) that integrates multiple symbolic similarity features (string, semantic, path) with GCN-based structural embeddings through iterative learning, eliminating the need for manually labeled alignment seeds.
limitations_reported: The multi-aspect symbolic characteristics contribute to entity alignment in different proportions, and the current approach uses fixed weights rather than automatically determining optimal feature integration; future work plans to introduce automatic feature engineering algorithms.
relevance_to_thesis: Reusable pipeline architecture; Transferable embedding component; KG/ontology component; Multilingual case; Evaluation metric/protocol; NeSy taxonomy/classification
free_notes: The framework is explicitly designed for unsupervised entity alignment, avoiding manual seed annotation. It demonstrates scalability by applying to multiple embedding models (AlignE, TransEdge, RTEA, RTEA_RA, GCN). Cross-lingual datasets involve Chinese, Japanese, French, and English, with entity name translation as a preprocessing step. The symbolic component (collective similarity) consistently improves over any single similarity feature as shown in ablation studies.

authors: Molka Tounsi Dhouib, Catherine Faron, Andrea G. B. Tettamanzi
title: Measuring Clusters of Labels in an Embedding Space to Refine Relations in Ontology Alignment
neural_component: Pre-trained fastText word embeddings (300-dimensional vectors trained on Wikipedia dumps for French and English) used to compute vector representations of entity labels and clusters of labels; cosine similarity used as the matching metric.
symbolic_component: OWL/RDFS/SKOS ontology structures parsed via rdflib and queried with SPARQL to extract lexical information (rdfs:label, skos:prefLabel) and structural information (rdfs:subClassOf, skos:broader hierarchies). Explicit rules (Equations 4 and 5) based on radius difference threshold (0.1) to classify relationships as equivalence (closeMatch) or hierarchical (narrowMatch/broadMatch).
symbolic_inference_evidence: At inference time, SPARQL queries extract hierarchical structural information from the ontologies to form clusters of entity labels. After neural cosine similarity identifies candidate matching pairs above a threshold, symbolic rules comparing the radii of embedding clusters (Equations 4 and 5) determine whether the relationship is equivalence or subsumption. The output correspondences are expressed as owl:sameAs, rdfs:subClassOf, skos:closeMatch, skos:broadMatch, or skos:narrowMatch properties.
component_interaction: Step 1: Symbolic extraction of lexical and structural information from ontologies via SPARQL queries over RDF/OWL/SKOS structures. Step 2: Neural computation of fastText word embedding representations for entities and clusters. Step 3: Neural cosine similarity identifies candidate correspondences above a threshold. Step 4: Symbolic rules compare cluster radii (derived from embeddings but governed by symbolic structural hierarchy) to refine relationships into equivalence or hierarchical types.
datasets: [{"name": "OAEI Conference Complex Alignment Benchmark", "size": "57 correspondences, 5 ontologies (cmt: 30 classes, conference: 60, confOf: 39, edas: 104, ekaw: 74)", "domain": "conference organization"}, {"name": "Silex IT Sector (ESCO-ROME, ESCO-Cigref, NAF-UNSPSC)", "size": "ESCO: 160 concepts, ROME: 117, Cigref: 42, NAF: 53, UNSPSC: 153", "domain": "IT skills, occupations, business sectors"}, {"name": "ONISEP-ROME", "size": "ONISEP: 5325 concepts, ROME: 12255 concepts, gold standard: 290 links", "domain": "occupations/education"}]
metrics_best_result: F1 = 0.27 on OAEI benchmark (standard); F1 = 0.97 on NAF-UNSPSC (authors' evaluation method); F1 = 0.59 on ESCO-Cigref (standard)
baseline_gap: +8 pp (on OAEI benchmark, standard evaluation)
explainability_mechanism: The system produces explicit symbolic rules (Equations 4 and 5) that determine relationship types based on radius differences of embedding clusters. The radius measure and cosine similarity scores are transparent and traceable, providing interpretable criteria for why a correspondence is classified as equivalence or hierarchical.
explainability_evaluation: N/A
hitl_details: The system is described as designed to support end-users by presenting a list of k best possible matches for expert validation (Silex and ONISEP use cases), but no formal HITL feedback loop is integrated into the algorithmic process.
specific_sources: fastText pre-trained word vectors (French: 1,152,449 tokens; English: 1M tokens, trained on Wikipedia); OWL/RDFS/SKOS ontology structures (OAEI conference ontologies, ESCO, ROME, Cigref, NAF, UNSPSC, ONISEP)
frameworks_libraries: rdflib, SPARQL, fastText
main_contribution: A novel ontology alignment approach that combines fastText word embeddings with a radius measure of label clusters derived from ontology hierarchical structure, enabling the discovery and refinement of both equivalence and hierarchical (subsumption) relations between entities.
limitations_reported: Cosine similarity may be too low for semantically related but lexically dissimilar entities; system cannot detect hierarchical relations between leaf nodes (no structural information to compute radius); radius threshold may misclassify equivalence vs. hierarchical relations; quality depends on the embedding model and its coverage of the domain vocabulary; performance gap between equivalence and subsumption detection on OAEI benchmark due to highly specific labels.
relevance_to_thesis: Reusable pipeline architecture; Transferable embedding component; KG/ontology component; Multilingual case; Explainability mechanism; Evaluation metric/protocol
free_notes: The paper handles French and English datasets using separate fastText models. The authors define a secondary evaluation method (top-k list) beyond standard P/R/F1 which significantly inflates results. The rules for relationship refinement (Eqs. 4-5) use a fixed 0.1 threshold for radius difference, which the authors acknowledge needs empirical optimization.

authors: Huan Liu
title: Attribute-Relationship Joint Embedding for Knowledge Graph Entity Alignment
source: 2025 5th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Industrial Technology Applications (AIITA), IEEE
neural_component: Two-layer GCN for structure embedding with highway network; two-layer GAT (Graph Attention Network) for relation-aware entity representation; GCN-based attribute embedding using entity-attribute adjacency matrix; attention mechanism for attribute weighting
symbolic_component: Knowledge graph structure (relation triples and attribute triples) from two source KGs; pre-aligned entity pairs as seed alignment constraints; entity-attribute adjacency matrix derived from KG schema
knowledge_representation: Other: Knowledge graph triples (relation triples and attribute triples)
symbolic_inference_evidence: The KG structure (relation triples and attribute triples) guides the neural embedding process: relation triples define the neighborhood for GAT-based relation embedding, attribute triples define the entity-attribute adjacency matrix for attribute embedding via GCN, and pre-aligned entity pairs serve as training seeds for the margin-based alignment loss. At inference time, entity embeddings from both KGs are compared in the shared vector space to find aligned entity pairs.
component_interaction: First, a two-layer GCN encodes basic entity structure embeddings from the KG. Then, a relation-aware GAT computes relation embeddings from head/tail entity pairs and aggregates relation information into entity embeddings. Next, attribute embeddings are constructed using entity embeddings and an entity-attribute adjacency matrix with learned attention weights. Finally, the relation-aware and attribute-aware embeddings are concatenated (with weight β) to produce a joint entity representation, trained with a margin-based loss using pre-aligned entity seed pairs.
datasets: [{"name": "DBP15K (ZH-EN)", "size": "15000 pre-aligned entity pairs", "domain": "general (DBpedia)"}, {"name": "DBP15K (JA-EN)", "size": "15000 pre-aligned entity pairs", "domain": "general (DBpedia)"}, {"name": "DBP15K (FR-EN)", "size": "15000 pre-aligned entity pairs", "domain": "general (DBpedia)"}]
metrics_best_result: Hits@1 = 92.8 on DBP15K (FR-EN); Hits@1 = 84.2 on DBP15K (JA-EN); Hits@1 = 79.8 on DBP15K (ZH-EN)
baseline_gap: +2.6 pp (FR-EN), +5.7 pp (JA-EN), +6.5 pp (ZH-EN)
explainability_mechanism: N/A
explainability_evaluation: N/A
specific_sources: DBpedia (DBP15K dataset)
frameworks_libraries: TensorFlow
main_contribution: Proposes ARJE, a knowledge graph entity alignment method that jointly embeds attribute and relation information via GAT-based relation modeling and GCN-based weighted attribute aggregation to enhance entity representations for cross-lingual entity alignment.
limitations_reported: Not specified (the paper only mentions future work on how to effectively implement entity embedding).
relevance_to_thesis: Transferable embedding component; KG/ontology component; Multilingual case; Reusable benchmark; Evaluation metric/protocol
free_notes: The paper addresses cross-lingual entity alignment across Chinese-English, Japanese-English, and French-English KG pairs. The datasets (DBP15K) involve 4 languages. The ablation study demonstrates the individual contributions of the relation module and the attribute module. No explainability mechanism is described.

authors: Peng Wang, Yunyan Hu
title: Matching Biomedical Ontologies via a Hybrid Graph Attention Network
neural_component: Hyperbolic Graph Attention Network (HGAT) with BioBERT embeddings (dimension 512) as initial concept representations, two stacked hyperbolic graph attention layers producing 128-dimensional embeddings, multi-hop neighborhood aggregation with attention mechanism, contrastive matching loss jointly optimized with graph reconstruction loss.
symbolic_component: OWL/RDFS axiom-based rule reasoning for ontology enriching (enriching domain/range via subproperty inheritance, rewriting concept axioms such as owl:intersectionOf, owl:unionOf, owl:equivalentClass, enriching property axioms such as owl:SymmetricProperty, owl:TransitiveProperty, enriching owl:sameAs, extending properties in concept hierarchy). External UMLS used for concept and neighborhood augmentation. Stable Marriage algorithm for final alignment extraction.
symbolic_inference_evidence: The symbolic component operates in a preprocessing/enriching phase before the neural matching: (1) rule-based reasoning over OWL/RDFS axioms discovers hidden triples and enriches the ontology graph structure (e.g., nearly doubling isA triples in SNOMED from 105,624 to 203,942); (2) UMLS is used as external resource for concept augmentation (anchoring via string matching, transferring semantic descriptions) and neighborhood augmentation (adding missing structural relations); (3) after neural embedding and similarity computation, the Stable Marriage algorithm (a symbolic combinatorial optimization procedure) is applied over pairwise similarity scores to produce final one-to-one alignments.
component_interaction: The pipeline proceeds in two main phases: (1) Ontology Enriching (symbolic): rule-based reasoning over OWL axioms preprocesses and enriches the ontology graphs, and UMLS-based augmentation adds missing concepts and relations. (2) Ontology Matching (neural): BioBERT encodes concept labels into initial embeddings, hyperbolic graph attention layers aggregate neighborhood structure in hyperbolic space, similarity is computed via hyperbolic distance, and finally the Stable Marriage algorithm (symbolic) selects one-to-one alignments from the similarity matrix. The symbolic enrichment directly shapes the graph input to the neural component.
datasets: [{"name": "OAEI 2021 Large Biomedical Track - FMA-NCI", "size": "FMA: 78,988 concepts; NCI: 66,724 concepts", "domain": "biomedical"}, {"name": "OAEI 2021 Large Biomedical Track - FMA-SNOMED", "size": "FMA: 78,988 concepts; SNOMED: 122,222 concepts", "domain": "biomedical"}, {"name": "OAEI 2021 Large Biomedical Track - SNOMED-NCI", "size": "SNOMED: 122,222 concepts; NCI: 66,724 concepts", "domain": "biomedical"}]
metrics_best_result: F1 = 0.926 on FMA-NCI; F1 = 0.832 on FMA-SNOMED; F1 = 0.850 on SNOMED-NCI
baseline_score: F1 = 0.933 on FMA-NCI; F1 = 0.835 on FMA-SNOMED; F1 = 0.818 on SNOMED-NCI
baseline_gap: -0.7 pp on FMA-NCI; -0.3 pp on FMA-SNOMED; +3.2 pp on SNOMED-NCI
explainability_mechanism: N/A
explainability_evaluation: N/A
specific_sources: UMLS (external domain resource for concept and neighborhood augmentation), BioBERT (pre-trained biomedical language model for concept embedding), OWL/RDFS axioms (rule-based reasoning for ontology enriching)
frameworks_libraries: Python, PyTorch
main_contribution: Proposes BioHAN, a biomedical ontology matching framework that combines rule-based ontology enriching (via OWL axioms and UMLS augmentation) with hyperbolic graph attention networks to capture hierarchical structure and multi-hop neighborhood information for improved concept alignment.
limitations_reported: Only considers the subClassOf relationship, which restricts graph representation learning capability; matching efficiency on large-scale biomedical ontologies is not addressed; no consideration of other non-isomorphism relations or heterogeneous features.
relevance_to_thesis: Reusable pipeline architecture; Transferable embedding component; KG/ontology component; Reusable benchmark; Evaluation metric/protocol
free_notes: Ablation study covers four variants: w/o ontology enriching (OB), w/o hyperbolic projection (HB), w/o attention mechanism (AM), w/o multi-hop neighbors (MN). Seed alignments extracted from UMLS serve as training data. The attention mechanism mentioned in GAT could potentially serve as a trace-level explanation but is not discussed in that capacity by the authors.

authors: Tingting Jiang, Chenyang Bu, Yi Zhu, Xindong Wu
title: Combining embedding-based and symbol-based methods for entity alignment
neural_component: Hybrid KG embedding model based on TransE that learns entity and relation embeddings from both fact triples and logical rules; also tested with RDGCN (graph convolutional network) as alternative embedding model
symbolic_component: Edit distance similarity, longest common subsequence, neighbor string similarity, machine translation for cross-language mapping, common substring comparison, rule extraction via AMIE+, ontology-based rule mining from RDFS (rdfs:subPropertyOf) and OWL (owl:equivalentProperty)
symbolic_inference_evidence: Symbolic components operate at multiple inference stages: (1) In Stage I (relation alignment), symbol-based methods (edit distance, common substring, owl:equivalentProperty, machine translation) identify aligned relation pairs that are injected into the embedding model via relation exchange; (2) In Stage II (entity alignment), the embedding model first generates candidate entity sets (top-k nearest neighbors in embedding space), then symbol-based methods (edit distance similarity and neighbor string similarity) re-rank/sort candidates to identify final aligned entity pairs; (3) Logical rules extracted from RDFS ontology knowledge bases and AMIE+ are embedded alongside fact triples in the hybrid embedding loss function.
component_interaction: Two-stage pipeline: Stage I uses symbol-based methods (edit distance, common substring, OWL equivalentProperty, machine translation) and optionally embedding-based methods for relation alignment; aligned relations are injected into the embedding model via relation exchange. Stage II uses the hybrid embedding model (trained on facts + logical rules) to generate candidate entity sets, then symbol-based string similarity and neighbor similarity methods re-rank candidates to produce final aligned entity pairs. Rules extracted from ontology knowledge bases (RDFS) and AMIE+ are jointly embedded with fact triples in the loss function.
datasets: [{"name": "DBP15K (ZH-EN)", "size": "~100,000 triples, 15,000 aligned entity pairs", "domain": "general (DBpedia cross-lingual)"}, {"name": "DBP15K (JA-EN)", "size": "~100,000 triples, 15,000 aligned entity pairs", "domain": "general (DBpedia cross-lingual)"}, {"name": "DBP15K (FR-EN)", "size": "~100,000 triples, 15,000 aligned entity pairs", "domain": "general (DBpedia cross-lingual)"}, {"name": "DWY100K (DBP-WD)", "size": "~900,000 triples, 100,000 aligned entity pairs", "domain": "general (DBpedia-Wikidata)"}, {"name": "DWY100K (DBP-YG)", "size": "~900,000 triples, 100,000 aligned entity pairs", "domain": "general (DBpedia-YAGO)"}]
metrics_best_result: RDGCN+SCE achieves Hits@1 = 93.26% on DBP FR-EN; ESEA achieves Hits@1 = 96.39% on DBP-YG (MRR = 0.965)
baseline_gap: +4.62 pp (RDGCN+SCE vs RDGCN on DBP FR-EN)
explainability_mechanism: The authors argue that using symbol-based methods (string similarity, neighbor similarity) to produce final alignment results retains interpretability, as the symbolic similarity scores provide a traceable justification for alignment decisions, unlike pure embedding-based methods which lack interpretability. The aligned relation pairs from symbol-based methods also provide interpretable intermediate results.
explainability_evaluation: N/A — interpretability is discussed qualitatively as an advantage of the framework but not formally evaluated via user study or ablation
specific_sources: DBpedia (including RDFS schema with rdfs:subPropertyOf and OWL owl:equivalentProperty), AMIE+ (rule extraction tool), TransE-based KG embeddings, RDGCN embeddings, Google Translate (for cross-language relation mapping)
frameworks_libraries: Not specified
main_contribution: Proposes ESEA, a two-stage framework that combines KG embedding (with fact triples and logical rules) with symbol-based string similarity methods to improve entity alignment accuracy, efficiency, and interpretability across cross-lingual knowledge graphs.
limitations_reported: The authors do not explicitly list limitations in a dedicated section; implicitly, the framework depends on the quality of candidate entities from the embedding model, rule noise may affect performance (controlled by hyperparameter w), and the symbol-based re-ranking relies on entity label availability.
relevance_to_thesis: Reusable pipeline architecture; Transferable embedding component; KG/ontology component; Multilingual case; Explainability mechanism; Reusable benchmark; NeSy taxonomy/classification
free_notes: The paper demonstrates that combining embedding-based candidate generation with symbol-based re-ranking consistently improves Hits@1 across multiple embedding models (TransE, JAPE, RTEA, RDGCN), suggesting the strategy is model-agnostic. Cross-lingual datasets (Chinese-English, Japanese-English, French-English) are central to evaluation. The framework uses RDFS/OWL ontology axioms for rule extraction, which is a distinctive symbolic knowledge integration approach.

authors: Yuejia Xiang, Ziheng Zhang, Jiaoyan Chen, Xi Chen, Zhenxi Lin, Yefeng Zheng
title: OntoEA: Ontology-guided Entity Alignment via Joint Knowledge Graph Embedding
source: Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: ACL-IJCNLP 2021
neural_component: TransE-based entity embedding module for KG entities and relations; non-linear transformation-based ontology embedding for class hierarchy; membership embedding module with non-linear transformation mapping entity embeddings to ontology embedding space; alignment loss module with learnable translation matrix. Optional initialization with fastText multilingual word embeddings for surface information variant.
symbolic_component: OWL/RDF ontologies with class hierarchies (rdfs:subClassOf), class disjointness constraints (owl:disjointWith), membership relationships (rdf:type) between entities and classes; Class Conflict Matrix (CCM) encoding explicit disjointness, hierarchy-based distance, and entity-membership-based conflict degrees.
symbolic_inference_evidence: The symbolic component operates at multiple phases: (1) During training, the CCM (derived from ontology class hierarchy, explicit owl:disjointWith constraints, and seed mapping membership analysis) constrains the embedding via a confliction loss (LC) that pushes embeddings of conflicting classes apart; (2) The membership loss (LM) associates entity embeddings with ontology class embeddings during training; (3) At prediction/inference time, the weighted similarity score combines entity embedding cosine similarity with class embedding cosine similarity (Eq. 7), meaning ontological class information directly influences the ranking of candidate entity alignments.
component_interaction: Five modules are jointly trained via iterative co-training: (1) entity embedding (TransE on KGs), (2) ontology embedding (non-linear transformation on class hierarchy), (3) confliction loss incorporating the symbolically-derived CCM into class embeddings, (4) membership loss linking entity embeddings to ontology class embeddings, and (5) alignment loss bridging two KG embedding spaces via seed mappings. At each iteration, entity and ontology embeddings are optimized first, then confliction and membership losses, then alignment loss. At prediction, a weighted combination of entity and class embedding similarities produces the final ranking.
datasets: [{"name": "EN-FR-15K-V1", "size": "15,000 entity pairs", "domain": "general (DBpedia cross-lingual)"}, {"name": "EN-FR-15K-V2", "size": "15,000 entity pairs", "domain": "general (DBpedia cross-lingual)"}, {"name": "EN-DE-15K-V1", "size": "15,000 entity pairs", "domain": "general (DBpedia cross-lingual)"}, {"name": "EN-DE-15K-V2", "size": "15,000 entity pairs", "domain": "general (DBpedia cross-lingual)"}, {"name": "D-W-15K-V1", "size": "15,000 entity pairs", "domain": "general (DBpedia-Wikidata)"}, {"name": "D-W-15K-V2", "size": "15,000 entity pairs", "domain": "general (DBpedia-Wikidata)"}, {"name": "MED-BBK-9K", "size": "9,162 entity pairs", "domain": "medical/industrial"}]
metrics_best_result: H@1 = 0.901, H@5 = 0.981, MRR = 0.931 on EN-FR-15K-V2 (w/ SI); H@1 = 0.566, MRR = 0.678 on EN-FR-15K-V1 (w/o SI); H@1 = 0.517, MRR = 0.604 on MED-BBK-9K (w/ SI, +68.9% over best baseline)
baseline_score: RDGCN: H@1=0.847 on EN-FR-V2 w/ SI; BootEA: H@1=0.660 on EN-FR-V2 w/o SI
baseline_gap: +4.3 to +68.9 pp H@1 (w/ SI varies by benchmark); +3.3 to +11.7 pp H@1 (w/o SI varies by benchmark)
explainability_mechanism: The class conflict analysis provides interpretable information: the CCM explicitly represents conflict degrees between classes, and the class conflict ratio metric shows the proportion of false positive mappings involving class conflicts. This allows tracing why certain entity alignments are avoided (due to class disjointness or hierarchy distance). The weighted similarity score (Eq. 7) also provides a decomposition into entity-level and class-level similarity.
explainability_evaluation: The class conflict ratio analysis (Fig. 4) shows OntoEA reduces class-conflicted false positives from 42.2–55.7% to 0.3–3.0% on EN-FR benchmarks. No formal user study for explainability.
specific_sources: DBpedia ontology (via SPARQL endpoint), Wikidata ontology (via SPARQL endpoint), domain-specific medical ontology (expert-constructed for MED-BBK-9K), fastText multilingual word embeddings, PARIS ontology alignment system, owl:disjointWith and rdfs:subClassOf constraints
frameworks_libraries: OpenEA, AdaGrad optimizer, NVIDIA Tesla M40 GPU, Python
main_contribution: OntoEA is the first method to jointly embed KGs and their ontologies for entity alignment, using a class conflict matrix and membership relationships to enrich KG embeddings with ontological semantics and reduce false positive mappings caused by class conflicts.
limitations_reported: Only class hierarchy and class disjointness constraints are utilized from ontologies; other ontology semantics (e.g., property domain/range) are not exploited. Performance gain is more limited on benchmarks with dense KG structures or fewer ontology classes. For KGs with separate ontologies, ontology alignment quality impacts results (manual annotation outperforms automatic methods).
relevance_to_thesis: Reusable pipeline architecture; KG/ontology component; Multilingual case; Reusable benchmark; Explainability mechanism; Evaluation metric/protocol; NeSy taxonomy/classification
free_notes: The paper demonstrates that 42.2–55.7% of wrong predictions by baselines involve class conflicts, which OntoEA reduces to 0.3–3.0%. The framework is modular and allows substitution of the entity embedding module (TransE) with other methods like GCN or RSN. Benchmarks were extended with ontologies and membership relationships and made publicly available. Cross-lingual experiments cover English, French, and German.

authors: Abhishek Kumar
title: Semantic Alignment of Multilingual Knowledge Graphs via Contextualized Vector Projections
neural_component: Fine-tuned multilingual transformer models (LaBSE, XLM-RoBERTa, mBERT) generating 768-dimensional contextual embeddings of verbalized entity descriptions; cosine similarity for cross-lingual matching
symbolic_component: HermiT OWL reasoner used to infer logical axioms and sub-class hierarchy from RDF/OWL ontologies; domain type constraints for post-matching filtering; template-based verbalization using rdfs:subClassOf and owl:equivalentClass axioms
symbolic_inference_evidence: The HermiT reasoner operates at the preprocessing/enrichment phase to infer logical axioms and sub-class hierarchy before embedding generation. Template-based verbalization uses structural relations (rdfs:subClassOf, owl:equivalentClass) to create entity descriptions. Post-matching domain/type constraints filter out structurally inconsistent pairs (e.g., invalid class-to-class mappings).
component_interaction: The symbolic component (HermiT reasoner) first enriches entities by inferring implicit axioms and hierarchy from OWL ontologies. Ontological structure (parent classes, attributes, relations) is verbalized into natural language descriptions via templates. These descriptions are then encoded by multilingual transformer models into embeddings. Cosine similarity and the Hungarian algorithm produce candidate alignments. Finally, symbolic type/domain constraints filter the aligned pairs to remove inconsistent matches.
datasets: [{"name": "OAEI-2022 MultiFarm track", "size": "Not specified", "domain": "general"}, {"name": "DBpedia multilingual aligned subsets", "size": "Not specified", "domain": "general"}, {"name": "YAGO aligned subsets", "size": "Not specified", "domain": "general"}, {"name": "Wikidata aligned subsets", "size": "Not specified", "domain": "general"}]
metrics_best_result: F1 = 0.71 (Precision = 0.65, Recall = 0.78) on OAEI-2022 MultiFarm; F1 = 0.82 on full model ablation; F1 = 0.79 on EN-DE alignment
baseline_score: F1 = 0.55 (LogMap on EN-DE Table I); F1 = 0.68 (MTransE on EN-DE Table III)
baseline_gap: +11 pp (vs MTransE EN-DE Table III)
explainability_mechanism: The authors explicitly state: 'Our alignment system is not explainable and cannot tell why two entities are similar.' Future work mentions using XAI tools like attention or saliency maps.
explainability_evaluation: N/A
hitl_details: N/A. The authors mention including human-in-the-loop validation as future work.
specific_sources: LaBSE, XLM-RoBERTa (XLM-R), mBERT (pre-trained neural); HermiT OWL reasoner with RDF/OWL ontologies (domain ontology); DBpedia, YAGO, Wikidata (domain KGs); Wikipedia abstracts for contextual enrichment
frameworks_libraries: FAISS (similarity search acceleration), HermiT reasoner, 4×NVIDIA A100 GPUs, SentencePiece tokenization
main_contribution: A cross-lingual knowledge graph alignment framework (CIDER-LM) that enriches ontological entities through template-based semantic verbalization and HermiT reasoning, then aligns them using fine-tuned multilingual transformer embeddings with cosine similarity and threshold-based filtering.
limitations_reported: Dependency on data quality of sparse/noisy KGs; poor performance on low-resource languages with insufficient pretraining data; assumes one-to-one correspondences while real KGs have partial or one-to-many alignments; system is not explainable; billion-scale alignment remains challenging even with FAISS.
relevance_to_thesis: Reusable pipeline architecture; Transferable embedding component; KG/ontology component; Multilingual case; Evaluation metric/protocol
free_notes: The system is called CIDER-LM throughout the paper. The abstract claims 71% F1 and 16% increase from best baseline on OAEI-2022 MultiFarm. Language pairs evaluated: EN-DE, EN-FR, EN-JA. The authors explicitly acknowledge lack of explainability and HITL as future work. The paper self-labels as 'Neuro-symbolic integration' in its index terms. The HermiT reasoner's contribution is quantified in ablation: removing it drops F1 from 0.68 to 0.64 on EN-JA.

authors: Chuanhao Xu, Jingwei Cheng, Fu Zhang
title: NALA: an Effective and Interpretable Entity Alignment Method
source: Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: EMNLP 2024
neural_component: BERT (fine-tuned) used to generate entity name/description embeddings and attribute value embeddings (768-dim CLS reduced to 300-dim output); cosine similarity on these embeddings provides premises for logical inference paths (Type II path) and literal similarity for Type I path premises.
symbolic_component: Non-Axiomatic Logic (NAL) framework implementing three types of logical inference paths: Type I (entity alignment via relational triples and functionality), Type II (entity alignment via name similarity), Type III (relation alignment via induction). Fixed syllogistic inference rules (deduction, analogy, conditional deduction, induction, revision) with two-dimensional truth-values (frequency, confidence). Iterative similarity inference module aggregates conclusions via probabilistic revision rule.
knowledge_representation: Other: Non-Axiomatic Logic (NAL) term logic with inheritance, similarity, and implication copulas; KG triples reinterpreted as NAL inheritance statements with truth-values
symbolic_inference_evidence: The symbolic NAL inference operates at the core of the similarity inference module during each iteration. Type I paths perform syllogistic inference (deduction, analogy, conditional deduction) over KG triples, entity similarities, and relation inheritances to derive entity similarity conclusions with truth-values. Type III paths use induction rules over aligned entity pairs and KG triples to infer relation inheritance statements. Conclusions from multiple path instances are aggregated using the revision and probabilistic revision rules. The matching module (rBMat algorithm with swapping) then uses the expectation values of these inferred similarity statements to produce 1-to-1 alignment results. Evidence log files are generated recording which premises constitute each path instance, providing interpretability traces.
component_interaction: BERT embeddings provide cosine similarity scores that serve as premises (truth-values) for NAL inference paths. Specifically, Type II paths use BERT cosine similarity of entity names/descriptions as their conclusion's truth-value, and attribute value similarities from BERT serve as premise (5) in Type I paths. The NAL symbolic inference module then processes these neural-derived premises alongside KG structural information through fixed logical inference rules to derive entity and relation alignment conclusions. The system iterates: alignment results from one iteration feed back as premises for the next iteration's inference. In unsupervised settings, a bootstrapping strategy uses initial NALA alignment results to fine-tune BERT, then reruns NALA with improved embeddings.
datasets: [{"name": "DBP15K ZH-EN", "size": "~19k entities per KG, 15k ground truth pairs", "domain": "general (DBpedia cross-lingual)"}, {"name": "DBP15K JA-EN", "size": "~19k entities per KG, 15k ground truth pairs", "domain": "general (DBpedia cross-lingual)"}, {"name": "DBP15K FR-EN", "size": "~19k entities per KG, 15k ground truth pairs", "domain": "general (DBpedia cross-lingual)"}, {"name": "DBP15K full ZH-EN", "size": "~66k and ~98k entities", "domain": "general (DBpedia cross-lingual)"}, {"name": "DBP15K full JA-EN", "size": "~65k and ~95k entities", "domain": "general (DBpedia cross-lingual)"}, {"name": "OpenEA D-W-15K-V1", "size": "15k entities", "domain": "general (DBpedia-Wikidata)"}, {"name": "OpenEA D-W-100K-V1", "size": "100k entities", "domain": "general (DBpedia-Wikidata)"}, {"name": "OpenEA D-Y-15K-V1", "size": "15k entities", "domain": "general (DBpedia-YAGO)"}, {"name": "OpenEA D-Y-100K-V1", "size": "100k entities", "domain": "general (DBpedia-YAGO)"}, {"name": "OpenEA D-W-15K-V2", "size": "15k entities", "domain": "general (DBpedia-Wikidata)"}, {"name": "OpenEA D-W-100K-V2", "size": "100k entities", "domain": "general (DBpedia-Wikidata)"}, {"name": "OpenEA D-Y-15K-V2", "size": "15k entities", "domain": "general (DBpedia-YAGO)"}, {"name": "OpenEA D-Y-100K-V2", "size": "100k entities", "domain": "general (DBpedia-YAGO)"}]
metrics_best_result: Hits@1 = 0.999 on DBP15K FR-EN (setting group 4, supervised with name+description+translator); Hits@1 = 0.998 on DBP15K ZH-EN (setting group 4); Hits@1 = 0.997 on DBP15K JA-EN (setting group 4)
baseline_gap: +3.0/+3.3/+0.4 pp on ZH-EN/JA-EN/FR-EN
explainability_mechanism: NALA is designed to be inherently interpretable. It generates an evidence log file recording the premises and inference steps (Type I, II, III paths) that lead to each alignment conclusion. Each inference path instance documents which KG triples, entity similarities, and relation inheritances constitute the premises, along with their truth-values (frequency and confidence). The authors distinguish their approach from post-hoc explainability: NALA is self-explanatory because the logical inference steps and their evidential basis are transparent by design. The two-dimensional truth-values (frequency, confidence) provide additional interpretive information about each conclusion.
explainability_evaluation: No formal user study or quantitative evaluation of explainability. The authors report that inspecting the evidence log file aided their own development process (troubleshooting faulty alignments). Generated evidences are displayed in the GitHub repository. The paper also provides qualitative analysis explaining how NALA's logical framework can explain the mechanisms of other EA methods (BERT-INT, FGWEA, TransE, MTransE).
specific_sources: BERT (pre-trained, fine-tuned on seed alignments for entity name/description embedding); DBpedia KG (structural triples, attribute triples); Google Translator (for cross-lingual entity name translation)
frameworks_libraries: Java (main NALA implementation), Python with PyTorch (BERT unit), NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 GPU
main_contribution: Proposes NALA, an interpretable entity alignment method that formalizes similarity inference using Non-Axiomatic Logic (NAL) with three types of logical inference paths, achieving state-of-the-art Hits@1 (0.98+) on DBP15K across supervised and unsupervised settings.
limitations_reported: Slightly more hyper-parameters than some EA methods; higher computational cost than fastest methods (1680s vs 34.5s for LightEA-I on D-Y-100K-V2) due to inability to utilize GPU in logical design; moderate performance when both attribute triples and entity name information are unavailable (pure structure setting).
relevance_to_thesis: Reusable pipeline architecture; Transferable embedding component; Explainability mechanism; Multilingual case; Reusable benchmark; NeSy taxonomy/classification; Evaluation metric/protocol
free_notes: The system uses Non-Axiomatic Logic (NAL), a term logic designed for AI under insufficient knowledge assumptions, as its symbolic reasoning framework. The neural component (BERT) is used minimally — only for literal similarity computation — while the symbolic inference paths do the heavy lifting for alignment. The paper provides an extensive theoretical analysis relating NALA's logical framework to other EA methods (TransE, MTransE, BERT-INT, FGWEA). Cross-lingual alignment is handled across Chinese-English, Japanese-English, and French-English language pairs. The iterative bootstrapping strategy allows unsupervised operation.

authors: Ernesto Jiménez-Ruiz, Asan Agibetov, Jiaoyan Chen, Matthias Samwald, Valerie Cross
title: Dividing the Ontology Alignment Task with Semantic Embeddings and Logic-based Modules
neural_component: StarSpace neural embedding model (TagSpace training setting) learns d-dimensional vector embeddings for words and ontology entities from the inverted lexical index (LexI). K-means clustering is applied on the resulting embeddings to partition LexI entries into clusters.
symbolic_component: Bottom-locality modules (logic-based ontology module extraction) compute self-contained ontology fragments that preserve the semantics of an input signature. OWL 2 ontologies serve as input. The inverted lexical index (LexI) provides structured indexing of ontology vocabulary with stemming and stop-word removal.
symbolic_inference_evidence: Locality modules operate after the neural clustering step: once LexI entries are clustered by the neural embeddings, each cluster's candidate mapping set is used as a seed signature for extracting bottom-locality modules from O1 and O2. These modules define the matching subtasks by guaranteeing self-contained, semantically complete ontology fragments. The symbolic module extraction thus shapes the final matching subtasks at inference time, ensuring coverage and semantic completeness of each subtask.
component_interaction: The pipeline proceeds as follows: (1) Ontologies O1 and O2 are indexed into a semantic inverted index LexI using symbolic lexical processing (stemming, stop-word removal, label splitting). (2) StarSpace neural embedding model learns vector representations for words and entities in LexI. (3) Embeddings of LexI entries are computed by concatenating mean word vectors and mean entity vectors. (4) K-means clustering partitions LexI entries into n clusters. (5) Each cluster derives candidate mapping sets, and bottom-locality modules extract self-contained ontology fragments as matching subtasks. The neural component (embedding + clustering) feeds the symbolic component (locality module extraction).
datasets: [{"name": "OAEI Anatomy (AMA-NCIA)", "size": "2,744 + 3,304 classes", "domain": "biomedical (anatomy)"}, {"name": "OAEI LargeBio FMA-NCI", "size": "78,989 + 66,724 classes", "domain": "biomedical"}, {"name": "OAEI LargeBio FMA-SNOMED", "size": "78,989 + 306,591 classes", "domain": "biomedical"}, {"name": "OAEI LargeBio SNOMED-NCI", "size": "306,591 + 66,724 classes", "domain": "biomedical"}, {"name": "OAEI Phenotype HPO-MP", "size": "11,786 + 11,721 classes", "domain": "biomedical (phenotype)"}, {"name": "OAEI Phenotype DOID-ORDO", "size": "9,248 + 12,936 classes", "domain": "biomedical (rare disease)"}]
metrics_best_result: Coverage ratio > 0.94 for reference alignments across all tasks; enabled ALOD2vec to achieve F1 = 0.751 on FMA-NCI (with 20 subtasks); FCA-Map achieved F1 = 0.749 on FMA-NCI (with 20 subtasks); size ratio reduced to as low as 5% of original task for FMA-NCI and FMA-SNOMED
baseline_gap: +18 to +35 pp coverage improvement over best prior partitioning methods on LargeBio tasks
explainability_mechanism: N/A
explainability_evaluation: N/A
specific_sources: OWL 2 ontologies (FMA, NCI Thesaurus, SNOMED CT, HPO, MP, DOID, ORDO, Adult Mouse Anatomy); StarSpace neural embedding model trained on task-specific inverted lexical index; UMLS Metathesaurus used as source of reference alignments for LargeBio track
frameworks_libraries: StarSpace (Facebook Research), SEALS evaluation infrastructure, Java, Python
main_contribution: A novel framework combining neural embeddings (StarSpace) and logic-based locality modules to accurately divide large ontology alignment tasks into smaller, self-contained matching subtasks while preserving high coverage (>94%) of reference alignments.
limitations_reported: Division requires specifying the number of subtasks n as input; the lexical index approach shares limitations with state-of-the-art systems when ontologies are lexically disparate or multilingual; increasing the number of divisions may slightly decrease coverage; global F-measure results are lower than top OAEI systems since evaluated systems were not fine-tuned; evaluation was conducted sequentially without parallelization.
relevance_to_thesis: Reusable pipeline architecture; Transferable embedding component; KG/ontology component; Reusable benchmark; Evaluation metric/protocol
free_notes: The paper does not perform ontology alignment itself but rather provides a pre-processing framework to enable systems that cannot cope with large ontologies. Five OAEI systems (MAMBA, FCA-Map, SANOM, POMap++, ALOD2vec) that previously failed on large tasks were enabled to produce results. Data and computed matching subtasks are available via Zenodo (doi:10.5281/zenodo.3547888). The approach defines formal quality measures (coverage ratio, size ratio) for evaluating matching task divisions.

authors: Yuan He, Jiaoyan Chen, Denvar Antonyrajah, Ian Horrocks
title: BERTMap: A BERT-Based Ontology Alignment System
neural_component: Bio-Clinical BERT (pre-trained on biomedical and clinical corpora) fine-tuned as a binary synonym/non-synonym classifier with a downstream linear layer and softmax; sub-word inverted indices based on BERT's WordPiece tokenizer for candidate selection
symbolic_component: Ontology class hierarchy (superclass/subclass relations) used for iterative mapping extension (locality principle); propositional logic-based mapping repair that removes mappings causing logical incoherence after ontology integration; sibling-class disjointness assumption for generating hard non-synonyms
symbolic_inference_evidence: At inference time, the symbolic component operates in two post-prediction pipeline phases: (1) Mapping Extension — the ontology class hierarchy (superclass/subclass structure) is traversed iteratively from high-confidence predicted mappings to discover new candidate mappings among neighbouring classes (Algorithm 1); (2) Mapping Repair — a propositional logic-based repair module (from Jiménez-Ruiz et al. 2013) checks logical coherence of the merged ontologies and removes mappings that lead to unsatisfiable classes. Additionally, during corpus construction (pre-inference), sibling-class disjointness inferred from the ontology structure is used to generate hard non-synonym training samples.
component_interaction: First, synonym/non-synonym corpora are extracted from ontology annotation properties and structural relations (intra-ontology, cross-ontology, complementary). BERT is fine-tuned on these corpora. At prediction time, sub-word inverted indices select candidate class pairs, the fine-tuned BERT classifier scores them. Then symbolic modules operate sequentially: mapping extension uses ontology hierarchy to recall additional mappings from neighbours of high-confidence predictions, and mapping repair uses logic-based reasoning to remove inconsistency-causing mappings.
datasets: [{"name": "OAEI LargeBio Track FMA-SNOMED (small fragments)", "size": "10,157 source classes, 13,412 target classes, 6,026 reference mappings", "domain": "biomedical"}, {"name": "OAEI LargeBio Track FMA-NCI (small fragments)", "size": "3,696 source classes, 6,488 target classes, 2,686 reference mappings", "domain": "biomedical"}, {"name": "FMA-SNOMED+ (extended with latest SNOMED labels)", "size": "Same class counts as FMA-SNOMED with additional labels", "domain": "biomedical"}]
metrics_best_result: F1 = 0.893 on FMA-NCI (unsupervised, io+ids with ex+rp, 90% Test); F1 = 0.836 on FMA-SNOMED (semi-supervised, io+co+ids+cp with ex+rp, 70% Test); F1 = 0.886 on FMA-SNOMED+ (unsupervised, io+ids with ex+rp, 90% Test)
baseline_gap: -2.5 pp on FMA-NCI; +1.4 pp on FMA-SNOMED; +2.5 pp on FMA-SNOMED+ (unsupervised, 90% Test)
explainability_mechanism: Mapping scores from the BERT classifier are provided for each predicted mapping (synonym probability P(c≡c')), and the system distinguishes between string-matched mappings (score=1.0) and BERT-predicted mappings. The mapping repair module provides implicit traceability by identifying and removing logically inconsistent mappings. Examples of correctly predicted mappings with implicit semantic connections (e.g., 'posterior' vs 'dorsal') are presented qualitatively.
explainability_evaluation: Qualitative examples of reference mappings correctly predicted by BERTMap but missed by LogMap and AML are presented (Table 5); no formal user study on explainability.
specific_sources: Bio-Clinical BERT (pre-trained neural model from Alsentzer et al. 2019), FMA ontology, SNOMED CT ontology, NCI Thesaurus ontology (domain ontologies serving as both alignment targets and knowledge sources for corpus construction), latest SNOMED version as complementary source
frameworks_libraries: owlready2 (ontology processing), Hugging Face transformers (BERT fine-tuning), PyTorch (implied), GTX 1080Ti GPU
main_contribution: Proposes BERTMap, a novel ontology alignment system that combines BERT fine-tuning on synonym/non-synonym corpora extracted from ontologies with ontology structure-based mapping extension and logic-based mapping repair, achieving state-of-the-art performance on OAEI LargeBio biomedical tasks in both unsupervised and semi-supervised settings.
limitations_reported: BERTMap does not outperform LogMap and AML on the FMA-NCI task (attributed to needing a more suitable mapping refinement strategy); evaluation limited to biomedical ontologies; future work needed on large-scale industrial data, BERT-based ontology embeddings, and tighter integration of prediction, extension, and repair.
relevance_to_thesis: Reusable pipeline architecture; Transferable embedding component; KG/ontology component; Reusable benchmark; Evaluation metric/protocol; NeSy taxonomy/classification
free_notes: The system supports unsupervised (intra-ontology corpus only) and semi-supervised (with cross-ontology corpus from known mappings) settings. The mapping repair module uses the propositional logic-based tool from Jiménez-Ruiz et al. (2013). Ablation covers corpus composition variants (io, ids, co, cp), mapping set types (src2tgt, tgt2src, combined), threshold sensitivity, and extension/repair contribution. The paper demonstrates that contextual embeddings significantly outperform traditional lexical matching and non-contextual embeddings for ontology alignment.

authors: Teeradaj Racharak, Watanee Jearanaiwongkul
title: JSimELHExplainer: A Robust Java Library for Explainable Semantic Similarity for ELH Description Logic Ontology
source: ISWC 2025 Companion Volume, CEUR Workshop Proceedings
neural_component: Pre-trained word embeddings (embedding-agnostic: supports static embeddings such as Word2Vec and contextualized models such as BERT) used to compute cosine similarity between concept labels and role names when structural identity does not hold.
symbolic_component: ELH Description Logic ontology reasoning: TBox unfolding, description tree construction from ELH constructors, structural comparison via homomorphism degree computation over primitive and existential concept pairs, role hierarchy traversal via transitive closure.
symbolic_inference_evidence: At inference time, the ontology TBox is recursively unfolded to produce description trees of primitive concepts. Structural comparison recursively traverses matching nodes/edges of the two description trees. When concept or role labels differ, the embedding-based cosine similarity is invoked (Equations 3 and 6). The final similarity score aggregates structural correspondence weighted by tree depth and logical operators. Explanations are generated by identifying contributing primitive concept pairs, existential concept pairs, and embedding-space alignments.
component_interaction: The algorithm interleaves symbolic and neural processing: it recursively traverses the description trees (symbolic structure) and at each comparison node, if labels are not identical, it calls the embedding model to compute cosine similarity (neural). The final score is an aggregation that weights primitive concept similarity and existential concept similarity, both of which may involve embedding lookups. The explanation module traces which pairs contributed and which embedding alignments were used.
task: Ontology alignment; Other: Concept similarity measurement in DL ontologies
datasets: [{"name": "SNOMED CT (Clinical Finding and Procedure categories)", "size": "206 concepts sampled (0.5%) from each of two top-level categories", "domain": "biomedical"}, {"name": "Family ontology", "size": "Not specified", "domain": "general (family relationships)"}]
metrics_best_result: Qualitative validation: concept pairs from the same SNOMED CT category consistently received higher similarity scores than cross-category pairs. Example similarity scores reported for family ontology pairs (e.g., Son-SonInLaw = 0.97, SonInLaw-DaughterInLaw = 0.8).
baseline_gap: Not specified
explainability_mechanism: The system produces a 4-tuple explanation consisting of: (1) the homomorphism degree from one concept to another, (2) a list of contributing primitive concept pairs, (3) a list of contributing existential concept pairs, and (4) an embedding map showing which role or primitive concept pairs had their similarity computed via the embedding space. Explanations are available in both JSON and ASCII formats.
explainability_evaluation: Qualitative insight analysis on family ontology and SNOMED CT; no formal user study reported. Authors state future plans to validate usability by involving users/developers.
specific_sources: SNOMED CT (domain ontology), Family ontology (OWL), pre-trained word embeddings (Word2Vec, BERT mentioned as compatible)
frameworks_libraries: Java, OWL API (supports .owl and .krss files)
main_contribution: A Java library implementing a neuro-symbolic framework that combines ELH Description Logic structural reasoning with pre-trained word embeddings for explainable concept similarity computation, producing fine-grained explanations identifying contributing concept pairs and embedding-space alignments.
limitations_reported: Only supports ELH ontologies with unfoldable (acyclic) TBoxes; no formal user study for explainability validation yet conducted; evaluation is primarily qualitative with limited quantitative benchmarking against baselines.
relevance_to_thesis: Reusable pipeline architecture; Transferable embedding component; KG/ontology component; Explainability mechanism; NeSy taxonomy/classification
free_notes: This is a tool/system paper presented at ISWC 2025 companion volume. The theoretical framework was previously published in Racharak (2021, IEEE Access). The library is embedding-agnostic and guarantees polynomial-time execution. The explanation mechanism is a core design goal (native explainability), not a post-hoc addition.
